NoteThe several kokudaka — and which one the paper uses



A reader can reasonably ask why the revenue row lists five fields. They belong to two families:

Assessed yield — what the land was rated to produce. The Tokugawa cadastral surveys rated every domain in koku, and Hansei Ichiran reports that rating in several variants. The two that matter conceptually are omotedaka (表高) — the “face” figure registered with the shogunate, frozen at early-1600s levels, which fixed a domain’s rank, precedence, and military obligation — and naidaka (内高), the domain’s own internal figure from its later surveys, which by the 1860s usually ran well above the official omotedaka. Sōdaka (草高), gōdaka (合高 — gross plus reclaimed new fields), and gendaka (現高) are further variants of this assessed-capacity number.
Actual rice — genmai (現米): the rice that actually flowed as tax in 1869. Because a domain taxed only a fraction of its assessed yield, genmai runs far below any assessed figure.

The paper uses both, for different jobs. The composite kokudaka (kokudaka_iv), drawn from the assessed-yield family, is the paper’s predictor — its measure of a domain’s size and revenue base. Genmai is used only to compute the expected 1884 peerage rank under the Asami (1994) threshold rule. They are not competing measures of one quantity.
The gap between the official omotedaka and a domain’s real productive base is a standard theme of early-modern Japanese fiscal history (see Ravina 1999). The kokudaka-versus-genmai question as it bears on the peerage is the Asami-versus-Lebra debate walked through in the kazoku-rank chapter and the 19-deviations chapter.

title: 16  The variables
section: 16.2 The most important columns for the paper
text:
16.2 The most important columns for the paper

16.2.1 Revenue base — kokudaka (column kokudaka_iv)
The composite kokudaka — the maximum of the available Hansei Ichiran fields (sōdaka, gōdaka, omotedaka), then Kyūdaka kyūryō torishirabechō (旧高旧領取調帳, 1872 Meiji cadastral survey, hosted by the National Museum of Japanese History / Rekihaku) for the 13 domains absent from HI. This is the denominator in the paper’s headline retainers-per-1,000-koku fiscal-distress metric (see chapter 04b).

Coverage: 276/286
Range: ~2,000 to ~1,400,000 koku
Why composite: Hansei Ichiran reports different fields for different domains. Saga uses 貮口合 (= gōdaka); Chōshū uses sōdaka; some small domains only have omotedaka. The composite picks the most-comprehensive figure available.
The Rekihaku tier: 13 domains have no recordable kokudaka in HI vol 1 (仙台, 新発田, 杵築, 母里, 清崎, 足利, 須坂, 岩村, 対馬府中, 岩国, 岩崎, 松岡, 斗南). For these, the IV is sourced from the 1872 Kyūdaka kyūryō torishirabechō cadastral survey — summed per-village from the Rekihaku open-access database. This is a kusadaka-tier (actual rated land yield) metric, the same conceptual tier as HI sōdaka / gōdaka, restoring methodological consistency. For 仙台 specifically, the IV uses only post-Boshin retained territory (excluding 1869 carve-off prefectures 登米/胆沢/江刺/角田), giving 299,659 koku instead of the raw 974,010-koku all-territory figure.
Citation: a sibling source column records which field was actually used for each domain (hi_sodaka, hi_godaka, hi_omotedaka, rekihaku_kyudaka_1872).







NoteThe Sendai-Date case in detail



仙台藩 is the one case in the dataset where the pre-Boshin and at-haihan-chiken kokudaka diverge dramatically. The 1869 Boshin punishment was a full territorial confiscation followed by re-grant of 28万石 to the three-year-old heir Date Munemoto 伊達宗基 (Meiji 1/12/12 = 1869-01-24, per 仙台市史 通史編6 近代1, 2008; 栗原伸一郎『戊辰戦争と「奥羽越」列藩同盟』, 2017; Bakkalian, The Sparrow’s Dream, Pittsburgh PhD 2017).
This shows up in Hansei Ichiran itself: vol 2 p.116R carries an explicit 「草高等不詳細」 (“kokudaka and similar details unclear”) annotation directly under the ○仙臺藩 header. HI itself documented being unable to record Sendai’s kokudaka in 1870, almost certainly because the new post-Boshin cadastral was incomplete at the time of the survey — strong provenance for using a substitute kokudaka drawn from the 1872 cadastral.
The dataset carries four Sendai kokudaka tiers for transparency:








Column
Value
Interpretation




pre_boshin_omotedaka_koku (sensitivity)
620,057
Pre-Boshin formal omotedaka (Date Masamune’s 1601 投与, frozen)


rekihaku_kyudaka_raw_koku (sensitivity)
974,010
Pre-Boshin all-territory cadastral kusadaka (Rekihaku unfiltered)


kokudaka_iv (the paper’s IV)
299,659
At-haihan-chiken kusadaka (Rekihaku-filtered to retained territory)


wiki_omotedaka_koku (cross-ref)
280,000
Post-Boshin formal grant to Date Munemoto



The carved-off territory (974,010 − 299,659 = 674,350 koku, in the 1869-created 登米県 / 胆沢県 / 江刺県 / 角田県) matches the documented Sendai 62万→28万 punitive reduction almost exactly — the 1872 cadastral preserves the Boshin penalty’s territorial fingerprint via the 旧県 column.
A methodological caveat: the 旧領 column in 旧高旧領取調帳 uses different temporal-reference conventions across prefectures (per Rekihaku’s own documentation and Kimura Motoi 校訂, 1969-79). The Tōhoku volumes (where Sendai sits) treat 旧領 as the pre-Boshin Bakumatsu lord — so the carve-off filter is correct for Sendai but the convention should be re-verified for other regions before being applied elsewhere.




16.2.2 1884 kazoku rank (column kazoku_rank_actual)
The peerage rank awarded in 1884, one of 5 tiers.

Coverage: 284/286 (2 documented exclusions)
Distribution: 3 prince, 15 marquess, 34 count, 222 viscount, 10 baron
Citation chain: 浅見雅男『華族誕生 名誉と体面の明治 (Kazoku tanjō: meiyo to taimen no Meiji, “Birth of the Peerage: Honor and Reputation in Meiji”)』(1994) + Kanpō 第307号 (1884) + 華族大鑑刊行会編『華族大鑑 (Kazoku taikan, “Compendium of the Peerage”)』(1990) for princes
See the kazoku Rank chapter for the full story.



16.2.3 Treatment — Boshin alignment (column boshin_alignment_group)
Each domain’s side in the Boshin War (Boshin sensō).

Coverage: 286/286
Values: anti-bakufu (幕府) / pro-restoration, pro-bakufu, neutral
Distribution: 236 anti-bakufu, 40 pro-bakufu, 10 neutral
Citation chain: Esposito (2020) + 工藤威 (2002) + Beasley (1972). See the Boshin Alignment chapter.



16.2.4 Paper-relevant — Genmai-rule deviations (column kazoku_rank_deviation_genmai)
The 19 daimyō (大名) families whose actual 1884 rank diverged from the mechanical prediction.

Coverage: 19/286 (most domains land where the rule predicts)
Why this column exists: Asami (1994) argues the 1884 threshold operated on genmai (現米) — post-1869 tax-reported rice flow — not sōdaka/omotedaka. Applying the genmai rule, 19 cases show real deviations. These are the paper’s core sample.
See The 19 Deviations chapter.



16.2.5 Paper-relevant — Focal-han tier (columns is_focal_han_tier1, is_focal_han_tier2, focal_han_tier_source)
The two-tier focal-domain commendation issued by the haihan-chiken imperial decree on 1871/7/14.

Tier 1 (4 domains, is_focal_han_tier1): Sat-Chō-Saga-Tosa = 薩摩 / 長州 / 佐賀 / 土佐 — the four domains explicitly commended for having first proposed the 1869 hanseki hōkan (return of registers). Synonymous with is_focal_han (both columns kept for naming clarity).
Tier 2 (8 domains, is_focal_han_tier2): Tier 1 plus 尾張 (Owari/Nagoya, Tokugawa) + 鳥取 (Tottori, Ikeda) + 熊本 (Kumamoto, Hosokawa) + 徳島 (Tokushima, Hachisuka) — the four additional domains commended on the same day for implementation merit (hanseki ritsu-sei, establishment of the [post-Restoration] register system).
Why this column exists: the project’s reading of the haihan-chiken-day primary sources documents two tiers of imperial commendation, not just one. The eight Tier-2 domains together represent the political coalition that publicly underwrote the abolition — useful as an alternative control variable for analyses where the four-domain Sat-Chō-Saga-Tosa indicator might be too narrow.
Coverage: 100% (binary indicators populated for every domain). Tier 1 count = 4; Tier 2 count = 8.
Citation chain (per focal_han_tier_source): triple-confirmed by the haihan-chiken imperial decree (Meiji Shiyō vol 1 pp.27–28), Inoue Kaoru’s biography (vol 1 p.452, which gives the explicit list 熊本・名古屋・徳島・鳥取), and Kido Takayoshi’s diary (vol II p.65, 28–29 Aug 1871 entries, in the Brown & Hirota 1983 translation). See the Primary sources section: the abolition decree, Inoue Kaoru’s account, and Kido’s diary. A fourth-source corroboration is in Ōkubo Toshimichi’s contemporaneous diary and the 1928 editorial 【辨説】 commentary in the Ōkubo Monjo vol. 4.
Note on Ikeda ambiguity: Kido’s diary mentions “the Governors Ikeda” without specifying Tottori versus Okayama (both Ikeda lines). Inoue’s biography is explicit on Tottori (鳥取), which the project treats as definitive.

16.4 How to find anything

title: 17  The road to abolition
text:
17.1 The Boshin War and its punishments (1868–69)
By the summer of 1871 the Meiji government did something that, on its face, looks impossible: in a single day it abolished roughly 270 hereditary domains — the political units that had governed Japan for two and a half centuries — and almost no one resisted. To see why that was possible, and why it reads better as a bargain than as a conquest, you have to start three years earlier, with the war that created the opening and the half-measure that came after it.
The Boshin War (戊辰戦争, Boshin sensō, 1868–69) ended the Tokugawa bakufu (幕府, the shōgunate). The Boshin-alignment chapter covers who fought on which side and how those choices were scored sixteen years later; here the point is narrower — what the victors did with their victory.
In the twelfth month of Meiji 1 (December 1868 – January 1869) the new government issued its punishment decrees against the domains that had led the losing Northern Alliance (Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei 奥羽越列藩同盟). The terms were severe but bounded:
But the houses were not destroyed. Within weeks, reduced versions of them were put back on the map — often on land confiscated from other punished houses:
The Morioka-Nanbu heir was reseated on the southern strip carved out of Sendai; a Sakai cadet from the punished Shōnai house was granted 120,000 koku at Wakamatsu, the former Aizu castle town. The pattern is consistent: punishment meant reduction and relocation, not extinction. The government that defeated its enemies left them standing, smaller. That instinct — to accommodate the losing side rather than erase it — runs through the rest of the story, down to the count rank the head of the Sendai-Date house received in the 1884 peerage (see The settlement).

title: 17  The road to abolition
text:
Aizu (会津藩) — Matsudaira Katamori’s death sentence was commuted by one degree to permanent house arrest, as were the sentences of the lords of Kuwana and Jōzai.
Six Northern Alliance houses — Sendai, Shōnai, Morioka, Nihonmatsu, Shirakawa, and Nagaoka — had their fiefs confiscated.
Yonezawa (米沢藩) — the Uesugi house had 40,000 koku cut from its fief.



Day 17: Nanbu Toshikatsu’s son is permitted to inherit the family rites, granted 130,000 koku of Iwaki territory, and relocated to Shiraishi.
— Meiji Shiyō, Meiji 1/12/17 (≈ 1869-01-29)








NoteWhy the punishments matter for what came later



A government that annihilates its defeated rivals teaches every surviving rival to fight to the death next time. A government that reduces and reseats them teaches the opposite: that submitting is survivable. The 1868–69 settlement set that precedent three years before the domains were asked to give themselves up entirely.

title: 17  The road to abolition
text:
17.2 The half-measure: returning the registers (1869)
The first attempt to dismantle the domains came in 1869, and it did not go all the way. In the return of the registers (hanseki hōkan 版籍奉還), the four leading domains — Satsuma, Chōshū, Tosa, and Saga — formally surrendered their land-and-population registers to the emperor, and most other domains followed. In principle, this transferred sovereignty over land and people to the throne.
In practice it changed the title, not the power. The daimyō (大名) were immediately reappointed as governors (chiji 知事) of the very domains they had just surrendered. They kept governing their own territory, collecting their own taxes, and commanding their own retainers. The registers had been “returned,” but the men who had held them were still in charge.
That gap — formal surrender, intact local power — is what the 1871 abolition closed. As Kido Takayoshi later recalled, the government had nearly settled for making the lords hereditary governors, an arrangement that would have frozen the half-measure in place; it took a deliberate fight to strike the word “hereditary” out (see The bargain of 1871).

title: 17  The road to abolition
text:
17.3 The fiscal vise
Underneath the politics, both sides were running out of money — and that shared insolvency is what turned an awkward standoff into a deal worth making.
The domains were buried under their own retainers. A daimyō owed hereditary stipends to a samurai class that, in many places, had grown far larger than the tax base could carry. The fiscal-distress chapter shows the measure the paper builds on — retainers per 1,000 koku of revenue — and how heavy it had become for domains across the spectrum, from Kaga at the low end to Satsuma at the high end. Many domains were, in plain terms, insolvent.
The central government had the opposite problem: ambitions, debts inherited from the bakufu and the war, and almost no tax base of its own, because the taxes were still being collected by the domains. It could not fund a modern state without the revenue the domains were sitting on — and the domains could not keep paying their retainers out of that revenue much longer.
Two parties, each unable to sustain the status quo, each holding something the other needed. That is the opening the next chapter walks through: not a conquest imposed from the top, but a settlement negotiated under pressure. (The paper formalizes this as a Political Coase Theorem bargain; the chapters that follow stay with what the sources say and let that framework be the paper’s own contribution.)

Boshin punishment decrees: 修史局編『明治史要 (Meiji Shiyō)』本紀 vol. 1, Meiji 1/12 entries (book pp. 128–132) — the imperial decrees confiscating and reducing the Northern Alliance fiefs and reseating the heirs (Nanbu → Shiraishi, Sakai → Wakamatsu). NDL Digital Collections. Read the decrees →
Retainer-burden data: Hansei Ichiran (藩制一覧, 1870), as compiled in the project dataset — see The retainer burden.
Background: Umegaki, After the Restoration (1988); Beasley, The Meiji Restoration (Stanford UP, 1972).

title: 18  The bargain of 1871
text:
18.1 Domains volunteering, before the decree
On 29 August 1871 (Meiji 4/7/14) the emperor abolished the domains. The edict was read aloud, the governors were dismissed, and the political map of Japan was redrawn in an afternoon. But the edict was only the surface of something that had been assembled over the preceding year — by a small circle of architects inside the government, with the leading domains volunteering rather than being overrun. This chapter follows how the deal was built, and closes on the question David Crego’s notes put at the center of the paper: if four domains had the power to do this, why didn’t they simply become a new shogunate?
The standard picture of 1871 is top-down: the government in Tokyo decides, and the domains submit. The contemporary record complicates that picture. Through late 1870 and into 1871, domains were already coming to the central government on their own — asking it to take over.
The official daily record (Daijōkan nisshi 太政官日誌) shows the pattern. Domains petitioned to have their revenues reallocated, to borrow from the treasury, or — in a striking number of cases — to dissolve themselves into prefectures voluntarily. The project’s reading of the 1870–71 record finds on the order of two dozen reallocation petitions plus nine voluntary liquidations, beginning with a Kumamoto petition in the autumn of 1870 and accelerating through the summer of 1871. The petitioners were mostly not the four leading domains; they were small and middling houses across the country, signaling that they could no longer carry themselves.
This is the bottom of the bargain: by 1871 a large part of the daimyō (大名) class was not defending its sovereignty but trying to hand it back on survivable terms.

title: 18  The bargain of 1871
text:
18.2 The architects
The top of the bargain was a small group inside the government. By their own and each other’s accounts, the central figures were:

Kido Takayoshi (木戸孝允) — from Chōshū, the chief political architect of the abolition
Ōkubo Toshimichi (大久保利通) — from Satsuma, his operational counterpart
Inoue Kaoru (井上馨) — from Chōshū, in the Finance Ministry, working the implementation
Iwakura Tomomi (岩倉具視) — the senior court noble who had to sign off

The four leading daimyō — Shimazu Tadayoshi (Satsuma), Mōri Motonori (Chōshū), Nabeshima Naohiro (Saga), and Yamauchi Toyonori (Tosa) — supplied the formal cover by petitioning for the reform; the imperial edict would name exactly these four as having “first proposed” the return of the registers.
Kido’s diary is unusually candid about how the policy was actually pushed. He claims the idea of a county-prefecture system (gunken-sei 郡県制) as his own, and describes the argument he used to make confiscating the domains legitimate:

I contrived a stratagem by saying that the fiefs of the lords had been granted to them by the Tokugawa, an Imperial enemy. They had never been authorized under the Imperial seal; thus, I asked, how can the Empire stand unless it is made very clear that one’s highest loyalty is to the Emperor?
— Kido Takayoshi, diary, 29 August 1871 (trans. Brown & Hirota)

This is the legal-moral hinge of the whole settlement: the lords’ holdings were reframed as never having been legitimate property in the first place, since they came from a defeated enemy. That framing lowered what the state had to offer in exchange — the daimyō were not being bought out of secure property so much as compensated for surrendering claims whose legitimacy was now denied. Kido also notes the personal risk: through 1871 he heard “the misgivings and vilification even from samurai who were my fellow clansmen,” and many “advocated assassinating me.” This was contested, dangerous work, not a formality.

title: 18  The bargain of 1871
text:
18.3 The decree week
The corrected chronology shows how compressed the final move was. The cabinet reshuffle that put the coalition in place happened around 10 August 1871; the decree came nineteen days later. The decisive week was three days:

27 August — Saigō Takamori, Ōkubo, and Kido held a secret conference to settle the plan, then took it for ratification through two separate channels: Saigō and Kido to Prince Sanjō, Ōkubo and Kido to Lord Iwakura. Kido insisted on including Iwakura over an argument to keep him in the dark:

Originally the argument was advanced that we should not tell Lord Iwakura beforehand. As Lord Iwakura has been connected with all important matters since the time of the Restoration, I was reluctant not to tell him.

28 August — the proclamation was drafted in secret by the imperial secretaries.
29 August — at 2 p.m. in the Grand Hall, the edict was read to the governors of fifty-six in-Tokyo domains, who were dismissed from their offices on the spot.
30 August — the senior councilors of the remaining ~260 domains were summoned and notified.

The ceremony itself encoded the coalition’s shape. The imperial messages came in two tiers, which Kido records plainly:

An Imperial message to our Lord Governor of Chōshū, and to the Lords Shimazu, Yamanouchi, and Nabeshima stated that the abolition of the domains would be proclaimed today. As the Governor of Nagoya, the Governors Ikeda, Hosokawa, and Hachisuka, have submitted petitions requesting the reform, there were Imperial messages especially for them.

The first tier is the four leading domains — Satsuma, Chōshū, Tosa, Saga — credited for proposing the 1869 return of registers. The second tier adds four more houses (Owari, Tottori, Kumamoto, Tokushima) credited for helping build the new system. Eight domains, spanning the full status spectrum from a senior Tokugawa-related house to major outside lords, were publicly thanked — a wider circle than the famous four.
And at the human center of it is a petition-and-response moment in Kido’s own words. The Chōshū lord, Mōri Motonori — to whom Kido felt a personal debt — had petitioned the throne for the reform days earlier and now prostrated himself among the dismissed governors:

a man to whom my obligations are deeper than the seas and higher than the mountains, prostrated himself as one of the fifty-six domain governors, and listened to the Imperial Edict. At this I choked up… and my tears fell in spite of myself… For this purpose he submitted his petition to the Throne a few days ago. His act made me feel that I should make every effort to have it accepted.

The lord petitions; the architect works to get the petition accepted. That is the bargaining channel running in both directions at once, recorded by the man who built it.

title: 18  The bargain of 1871
text:
18.4 Why not a four-domain shogunate?
The four leading domains had the military strength to centralize on their own terms. The natural worry — David’s question — is why that strength didn’t simply produce a new hegemony, with Sat-Chō-Tosa-Hizen replacing the Tokugawa. The contemporary record points to four reasons the coalition could not, and did not, entrench:

They could not act unilaterally. Even the three core architects had to route the decision for formal ratification through both Sanjō and Iwakura on 27 August. Approval through the broader court was a procedural requirement, not a courtesy.
They disagreed among themselves. The architects had to negotiate their own differences — over the balance of the new cabinet, and over timing; Saigō, in particular, was the most cautious about moving when they did. A coalition that has to bargain internally cannot dictate externally.
The deal was broad by design. The bargaining channel reached dozens of non-focal domains through the petition record, the public commendation extended to eight houses, and the later peerage settlement would fold in non-focal elites as well (see The settlement). Buying in that many players is the opposite of monopolizing.
The outcome was contingent. The government had nearly settled for making the daimyō hereditary prefecture governors — a far weaker reform that would have left their local power intact. Kido fought it:

the Imperial Government reached an agreement to appoint the feudal lords as hereditary governors of the domains. In consequence, I argued as strongly as possible against that arrangement, and finally we eliminated the two characters for ‘hereditary.’

That a single word had to be struck out at the end shows how un-predetermined the result was.

The paper reads these four facts through the Political Coase Theorem: the same factional rivalry that made the coalition strong enough to threaten also made it unable to dominate, so commitment had to be purchased with shared institutions rather than imposed by force. That framework is the paper’s contribution; the record on its own simply shows a coalition that could not move without bringing others along.
What it bought the others is the subject of the next chapter.

The imperial decree: 修史局編『明治史要 (Meiji Shiyō)』本紀 vol. 1, Meiji 4/7/14 entry (book pp. 26–28) — names Shimazu Tadayoshi, Mōri Motonori, Nabeshima Naohiro, and Yamauchi Toyonori as first proposers, and commends Tokugawa Yoshikatsu, Hosokawa Morihisa, Ikeda Yoshinori, and Hachisuka Mochiaki. NDL Digital Collections. Read the decree →
Kido’s account (all quotations): The Diary of Kido Takayoshi, vol. II, trans. Sidney D. Brown & Akiko Hirota (University of Tokyo Press, 1983), book pp. 63–67 (entries of 27–31 August 1871). Read the diary →

Dates follow the corrected Gregorian conversions (27 → 29 → 30 August 1871).

title: 19  The settlement: bonds and peerage
text:
19.1 From stipends to bonds: the 1876 buyout
Abolishing the domains in 1871 did not end the bargain — it opened a thirteen-year process of making it stick. The daimyō (大名) and their samurai had given up sovereignty and, soon, their hereditary incomes. What they got back came in two forms: money, as government bonds, and status, as a hereditary peerage — both on terms the state could not easily walk away from. Those two instruments, the 1876 stipend buyout and the 1884 peerage, are what turned a one-day abolition into a durable settlement.
After 1871 the former lords and samurai were paid stipends out of the central treasury, replacing the domain revenues they had lost. That was expensive and open-ended, and in 1876 the government closed it out. The stipend settlement (chitsuroku shobun 秩禄処分) converted every hereditary and merit stipend into a one-time government bond (kinroku kōsai 金禄公債).
The decree (Daijōkan Decree No. 108, 5 August 1876) is blunt about its reach. It applied to everyone who held a stipend — “kazoku, shizoku, or commoner” (華士族及ヒ平民) — with no protected class. And the conversion was deliberately tilted:
The combined effect was compression: the biggest former-daimyō stipends got the least generous terms. The heads of the four leading houses, whose post-1871 stipends ran into the tens of thousands of yen, sat in the bottom tier — five to six years’ purchase at 5%. The settlement paid the old elite off, but it did not preserve the old distribution.
The bond’s structure mattered as much as its size. The principal was locked up for five years and then retired by lottery over thirty (Article 6): from the sixth year, the Finance Ministry drew bonds for redemption each year, so no holder could choose when to cash out, and no single year carried the whole fiscal load. A companion decree the same day (No. 109) went further and prohibited buying or selling the bonds at all, pending later instructions — a restriction not lifted until around September 1878. At the outset, holders were locked into the schedule.
This timing was fought over inside the government. Kido Takayoshi wanted the stipend buyout announced together with a peerage, so the two halves of the compensation would arrive as a package; Itō Hirobumi and Prime Minister Sanjō overruled him and ran the buyout first, framing the sequencing as a matter of “political tactics.” The peerage would not come for another eight years.
What makes the bonds durable is mundane but decisive: they were written into law, paid interest twice a year, and were folded into the government’s general public debt rather than held apart. Reneging on them would have meant defaulting on the state’s own borrowing. The paper reads this as the financial half of a credible commitment; on the face of the decree, it is simply a settlement the government had bound itself to honor.

title: 19  The settlement: bonds and peerage
text:
The size of the payout ran against the size of the stipend. A recipient with a very large stipend (≥70,000 yen) received a bond worth only five years’ purchase; a recipient at the bottom of the scale received as much as fourteen years’ worth.
The interest rate ran the same way — 5% on the largest bonds, rising to 7.5% on the smallest.

title: 19  The settlement: bonds and peerage
section: 19.2 From status to peerage: the 1884 ranks
text:
19.2 From status to peerage: the 1884 ranks
The other half arrived in 1884. The peerage ordinance (kazoku-rei 華族令) established the five ranks — prince, marquess, count, viscount, baron (五爵) — and sorted the old and new elites into them. The founding decree, issued on 7 July 1884, names two distinct grounds for ennoblement in a single breath:

the kazoku and meritorious lineages are the bulwark of the nation … civilian and military officials who have assisted the great enterprise of the Restoration, those who have rendered great service to the nation, should all be elevated.
— Kazoku-rei imperial decree, Kanpō No. 307 (1884)

The first clause is the lineage track: the former daimyō and court houses, ranked by who they were. The four leading houses landed at the top of it — Shimazu (Satsuma) and Mōri (Chōshū) as princes, Nabeshima (Saga) and Yamauchi (Tosa) as marquesses. How the lineage ranks were assigned, and the cases that deviate from the rule, are the subject of the peerage chapter and the nineteen deviations.
The second clause is the merit track: the men who had actually run the Restoration and the new state, ranked by what they had done. And here the continuity with 1871 is exact. Of the sixteen senior-merit counts created in 1884, all sixteen came from the four leading domains — seven from Satsuma, six from Chōshū, two from Tosa, one from Saga (Yamagata, Itō, Kuroda, Inoue, and the rest). The coalition that abolished the domains in 1871 staffed the top of the merit peerage in 1884.
The peerage was hereditary, and within five years it was load-bearing: the 1889 constitution gave princes and marquesses automatic seats in the House of Peers and let the lower ranks elect their own members. At that point the state could not strip kazoku status without dismantling part of its own legislature — which is the institutional reason the paper treats the peerage as a commitment the government could not cheaply reverse.
The settlement also reached the losing side. The head of the Sendai-Date house — a pro-bakufu Northern Alliance leader whose domain had been cut by more than half after the war — received a count rank in 1884, listed under the ordinary lineage formula alongside the non-penalized houses (see the Aizu–Sendai walkthrough). The same instinct visible in the 1868–69 punishments — reduce and reseat, don’t erase — closes the loop here: the bargain accommodated even the houses that had fought against it.

title: 19  The settlement: bonds and peerage
section: 19.3 The shape of the whole bargain
text:
19.3 The shape of the whole bargain
Put the three chapters together and the arc is one continuous settlement, not three separate events. The domains were insolvent and the state was revenue-starved (the road to abolition); a small coalition negotiated the surrender of sovereignty under pressure, unable to impose it without bringing others along (the bargain of 1871); and the surrender was made durable by paying the old elite in bonds and status that the state had bound itself to honor (this chapter).
That is the history the dataset measures. The retainer burden that made the domains insolvent is in the fiscal-distress chapter; the ranks that recorded the final settlement are in the peerage chapter; and the war that set the alignment is in the Boshin chapter. The chapters that follow turn the story into numbers.

The stipend buyout: 太政官布告 第108号・第109号 (Daijōkan Decrees Nos. 108 & 109), Meiji 9/8/5 (5 August 1876), “金禄公債證書發行條例,” in 『法令全書』明治9年 (book pp. 147–151). NDL Digital Collections, pid 787956 (PDM). Read the decrees →
The contested timing: The Diary of Kido Takayoshi, vol. III (trans. Brown & Hirota, University of Tokyo Press), entries of 3–7 August 1876 — Kido’s protest and Itō’s “political tactics” framing of the 1876 → 1884 sequence.
The peerage: 『官報』第三百七號 (Kanpō No. 307), Meiji 17/7/8 (8 July 1884) — the kazoku-rei imperial decree (dated 7 July) and the ennoblement roster, including the sixteen senior-merit counts drawn entirely from the four leading domains. NDL Digital Collections, pid 2943511 (PDM). Read the decree →

title: 20  Fiscal distress: the retainer burden
text:
20.1 The metric: retainers per 1,000 koku
The paper’s central use of Hansei Ichiran is not to predict who got which 1884 peerage rank. It is to document the structural fiscal crisis that made the 1871 abolition of the feudal domains (haihan chiken (廃藩置県)) a mutually advantageous bargain in the first place.
The Political Coase Theorem argument requires that both parties to a bargain perceive the gains from cooperation to exceed the costs of conflict. For the new Meiji government, the gain was monopoly over taxation and the military. For the daimyō (大名) — the roughly 280 feudal lords — the gain had to be large enough to outweigh their reluctance to surrender three centuries of inherited sovereignty. Hansei Ichiran lets us see why that gain was, in fact, large: by 1870 most domains were insolvent.
The headline statistic the paper uses is retainers per 1,000 koku — the number of hereditary samurai (侍) a domain owed stipends to, normalised by the domain’s revenue base (its kokudaka (石高)). It functions as a standardised measure of structural insolvency: how heavily a domain’s fixed, hereditary payroll obligations weighed on its productive capacity.
A higher ratio means more samurai mouths to feed for each unit of economic base — and because samurai stipends were a hereditary right chitsuroku (秩禄), not a discretionary expense the lord could trim, the ratio is closer to a fixed liability than a flexible cost. A domain at 100 retainers per 1,000 koku has roughly twice the per-koku stipend burden of one at 50.
The numerator is the count of samurai-class persons living off domain stipends (combining shizoku (士族), the regular samurai class — and sotsu (卒族), the foot-rank retainers). The denominator is the composite kokudaka from Hansei Ichiran, in thousands of koku.

title: 20  Fiscal distress: the retainer burden
section: 20.5 What this implies for the bargain
text:
20.5 What this implies for the bargain
By 1870, the typical daimyō was paying out hereditary stipends to ~5 percent of their formal koku base — a fixed obligation with no realistic prospect of relief through ordinary fiscal management. The 1871 haihan chiken offered each lord:

Debt relief. The state assumed the domain’s accumulated debts to merchants and rice-brokers.
A personal stipend of one-tenth of the domain’s revenue, paid by the central government (later converted to interest-bearing kinroku kōsai (金禄公債) bonds in the 1876 chitsuroku shobun (秩禄処分) — see the stipend buyout: regressive multipliers × progressive interest, 5-year grace + 30-year lottery amortization; Sat-Chō-Saga-Tosa former daimyō all sit in the least-generous conversion tier).
kazoku peerage status, eventually formalised as the 1884 five-tier system (see the peerage decree and The settlement).
Access to government office. The largest domains received sangi (参議, Imperial Councilor) appointments; the rest received genrōin (元老院, Senate of Elders) membership and other formal positions.

Set against the alternative — continuing to service a hereditary stipend payroll worth a third of national revenue, with no monopoly cash crops, no tax-collection apparatus, and the new Imperial Army next door — the bargain was lopsided in favour of acceptance. Only one domain head (島津久光, the Satsuma 国父) seriously objected, and as the paper notes (Ravina 2003), his retainer 大久保利通 had already taken the matter out of his hands.
That receptiveness is what Hansei Ichiran documents. The 1870 survey was not commissioned to predict 1884 peerage assignments. It was commissioned because the new government needed to know — in precise, per-domain detail — exactly how unsustainable the existing system was, so that the buyout could be priced and the political bargain could be made.

Citation chain for this chapter:
Primary data: Hansei Ichiran (藩制一覧), 1870, both volumes, NDL Digital Collections. Per-domain shizoku and sotsu person counts; per-domain composite kokudaka.
Supplementary primary: Meiji Shiyō, the Ōkuma 府藩県石高人口表 (Fu-han-ken kokudaka jinkō-hyō, “Prefecture-Domain-County Kokudaka and Population Table”) (Waseda Library イ14-A4361), and Tōkei Shūshi 人員表 (Jin’in-hyō, “Personnel Table”) for cross-validated population counts where Hansei Ichiran’s entries are partial.
Paper-published per-domain values: Reese & Crego (forthcoming), Historical Setting §2. Where the paper’s specific retainer counts diverge from the dataset’s shizoku+sotsu sum, the source is documented in the paper’s footnote 5 (ashigaru foot-soldiers excluded).

Banno, Japan’s Modern History 1857–1937 (2010) and Gordon, A Modern History of Japan (2002) for the aggregate national stipend burden of 18.78 million yen / ~33 percent of state expenditure in 1875.

title: 21  kazoku (華族) rank — the institutional side payment
text:
21.1 Why a peerage at all?
In the paper’s Political Coase Theorem framing, the 1884 kazoku peerage system is the side payment that made the 1871 bargain credible. The daimyō (大名) had surrendered fiscal sovereignty in 1871. By 1884 they had been disarmed, relocated to Tokyo, and stripped of any practical means to resist the central state. The kazoku peerage gave the new arrangement a stable institutional shape: hereditary noble status, formally derived from the Emperor, that the state could not later default on without dismantling its own ceremonial architecture.
This chapter walks the kazoku system — what it was, how the five ranks were assigned, and how it functioned as a credible-commitment mechanism in the broader bargain.
The new Meiji state in 1871 faced a credible-commitment problem. It had just assumed responsibility for paying the hereditary stipends of every former-daimyō family and tens of thousands of their samurai (侍) retainers — an obligation that, by 1875, consumed nearly a third of national revenue (Banno 2010; Gordon 2002). It had every short-term incentive to default once the daimyō were powerless to enforce the bargain. The question was: why wouldn’t it?
Three mechanisms together provided the answer:
The 1884 ranks are step 2 in that sequence — the moment the loose kazoku status acquired its precise institutional shape.
crumbs: 21  *kazoku* (華族) rank — the institutional side payment

title: 21  kazoku (華族) rank — the institutional side payment
text:
The 1869 founding of the kazoku status merged the former-daimyō with the existing kuge (公家) court aristocracy into a single national peerage formally derived from the Emperor. Status became Emperor-granted, not domain-derived, anchoring it to the regime’s own legitimacy.
The 1884 kazoku Law (華族令) formalised the five hereditary ranks with explicit assignment rules, converting the loose 1869 status into a structured institutional class with documented inheritance.
The 1889 Meiji Constitution wrote the kazoku into the House of Peers (貴族院, kizoku-in) — the upper chamber of the new legislature. From 1889 onwards, defaulting on kazoku status meant dismantling part of the state’s own legislative architecture.

title: 21  kazoku (華族) rank — the institutional side payment
section: 21.4 The plot twist — the threshold operates on genmai, not sōdaka (草高)
text:
21.4 The plot twist — the threshold operates on genmai, not sōdaka (草高)
The threshold rule above is usually summarised with sōdaka (sōdaka, the nominal yield assessment). Under that reading, 仙台藩 (Sendai, the Date family) at ~620,000 koku should have got marquess but received count — and that gap was traditionally read as a Boshin punishment for joining the Northern Alliance.
Asami (1994) shows the threshold was actually applied to genmai (現米) — the post-1869 tax-reported actual rice flow, which is much smaller than sōdaka for most domains. Sendai’s genmai was ~67,740 koku — squarely in count range. By the genmai rule, Sendai’s rank is mechanical, not punishment. Aizu’s post-Boshin remnant 斗南藩 (Tonami) is similarly mechanical: its genmai of ~7,380 koku put it in viscount range. The territorial reduction of Aizu was the punishment; the rank assignment just followed.
This re-baseline matters for any descriptive interpretation of the 1884 rank pattern. Under the sōdaka reading, 81 domains look like deviations from the rule. Under the genmai reading, only 19 do — and those 19 fall into a clean set of named exception clauses. Eleven are upgrades above the genmai threshold: eight are anti-bakufu Restoration-service rewards (薩摩 and 長州 to prince; 宇和島 and 福井 to marquess; 大村, 佐土原, 対馬府中, 津和野 to count), and three are pro-bakufu Tokugawa-lineage houses elevated for dynastic status rather than Restoration merit (水戸 Mito under the gosanke clause; 静岡 Shizuoka, the Tokugawa main house relocated after the Restoration, to prince; 龍岡, an Ōgyū-Matsudaira house, to count). The remaining eight are baron downgrades — the post-1868 elevations of former 付家老 / 旗本 houses. The data documents an institutional architecture, not a discretionary rewards-and-punishments scheme. The 19 cases are walked through in chapter 07.

title: 21  kazoku (華族) rank — the institutional side payment
text:
21.6 How to read this map

The purple cluster in Kyushu and at the western Honshu tip (薩摩, 長州) is the Restoration coalition that got the prince and marquess promotions.
The orange cluster in the north — 仙台, 米沢, 盛岡, the post-Boshin Aizu remnant 斗南 — is mostly former Northern Alliance members at count rank. Read together with the fiscal-distress data, the count rank for these houses is the side-payment side of the bargain: the post-Boshin territorial reductions had been the cost; the kazoku rank is the institutionalised compensation.
Light purple (marquess) is scattered — large tozama (加賀, 土佐, 佐賀, 熊本, 広島, 岡山, 福岡, 徳島, 秋田) plus the gosanke (尾張, 紀伊, 水戸).
Viscount is everywhere small — the 222 small-to-medium domains that cleared the threshold.
Brown (baron) is the 10 post-1868 elevations — former 付家老 (tsukegarō), 旗本 (hatamoto), and 交代寄合 (kōtai-yoriai) houses that became independent daimyō only at the Restoration. Their baron rank reflects pre-Restoration baishin (陪臣) status, not Boshin alignment.
crumbs: 21  *kazoku* (華族) rank — the institutional side payment

title: 21  kazoku (華族) rank — the institutional side payment
text:
21.7 The kazoku as credible commitment
The paper’s reading of the 1884 ranks is institutional, not predictive. The ranks themselves were the instrument of the political bargain — the visible, hereditary, Emperor-derived status that bound the former-daimyō families to the new regime’s success. By 1889 the constitutional architecture made this binding explicit: prince and marquess seats in the House of Peers were automatic and hereditary; count, viscount, and baron seats were elected from within each rank. The state could not default on kazoku status without simultaneously destroying its own upper chamber. That is the credible-commitment mechanism the paper identifies.

Primary record: Kanpō 第307号 (1884-07-08) and 第308号 (1884-07-09), NDL Digital Collections (pids 2943511 and 2943512) — the government gazettes publishing the ennoblement decree. Project verbatim transcription: the imperial decree text + 83-person merit-track ennoblement roster + 10 AM 1884-07-07 ceremony procedure are on the peerage decree page. The transcription documents two findings worth noting here: (1) the decree’s dual rationale (lineage track + merit track) is encoded in its founding text; (2) all 16 senior-merit (勳一等) Counts on 1884-07-07 were drawn from the focal-4 han (7 Satsuma + 6 Chōshū + 2 Tosa + 1 Saga), confirming the focal-han coalition’s continuity from haihan-chiken (1871) into the kazoku-rei (1884).
Sendai-Date in the register: 從五位 伊達宗基 received count rank in the Kanpō #307 roster under the standard lineage formula 「授伯爵　從五位　伊達 宗基」 — the same wording as the non-penalized houses, despite Sendai’s pro-bakufu Boshin record and post-war territorial reduction. This is consistent with Asami’s genmai-mechanical reading (the rank was not a separate punishment); see the 19 deviations chapter.
Scholarly synthesis: 浅見雅男『華族誕生 名誉と体面の明治 (Kazoku tanjō: meiyo to taimen no Meiji, “Birth of the Peerage: Honor and Reputation in Meiji”)』リブロポート (1994) — backs the assignment-rule reconstruction and the genmai / sōdaka distinction. 華族大鑑刊行会編『華族大鑑 (Kazoku taikan, “Compendium of the Peerage”)』(1990) — primary source for the prince-rank ennoblements. 小田部雄次『華族 近代日本貴族の虚像と実像 (Kazoku: kindai Nihon kizoku no kyozō to jitsuzō, “The Peerage: Image and Reality of the Modern Japanese Aristocracy”)』中公新書 (2006) — secondary kazoku reference. 松田敬之『〈華族爵位〉請願人名辞典 (Kazoku shakui seigan jinmei jiten, “Dictionary of Peerage-Title Petitioners”)』吉川弘文館 (2015) — definitive on 1884 exclusions.
Actor perspective on chitsuroku→kazoku-rei sequencing: Kido Takayoshi’s contemporaneous diary records his August 1876 opposition to chitsuroku-shobun timing and Itō Hirobumi’s overruling, attributing the eight-year gap (1876 → 1884) to Itō’s “political tactics” calculus (木戸孝允日記 vol. III, Brown & Hirota trans.). The 1876 decree itself is on the stipend buyout page; the narrative is in The settlement.
Institutional architecture: Beckmann (1957) on the 1889 Constitution and the House of Peers. Beasley, The Meiji Restoration (1972), ch. XI.

title: 22  Boshin alignment (the paper’s treatment variable)
text:
22.1 The two coalitions
The Boshin War (戊辰戦争, Boshin sensō, 1868–69) was the civil war that ended the Tokugawa bakufu (幕府, the shōgunate). Every daimyō (大名) family had to take a side. Sixteen years later, those choices mattered for the kazoku (華族) rank they received.

title: 23  Patterns in the 1884 rank assignments
text:
23.1 Why are there deviations at all?
This chapter documents a specific pattern in the dataset: 19 cases where the 1884 kazoku (華族) peerage rank a family received departed from what the 叙爵内規 (assignment rules) would have predicted from their domain’s revenue base alone. These 19 cases are not the paper’s central empirical finding — the paper’s argument is the Political Coase Theorem framing — but they are an interesting set of observations that illuminate the institutional architecture of the 1884 ennoblement, and they cluster cleanly with the paper’s reading of the kazoku system as institutional bargain rather than discretionary reward.
Under the naive sōdaka (草高) reading of the threshold rule, 81 domains in the dataset show “deviations” from the 1884 mechanical prediction. That’s a lot of deviations, and an earlier literature (Lebra 1993, for example) reads many of them as discretionary Boshin punishments and rewards.
Two examples were often cited under that reading:
The deviations-as-discretionary-punishment reading would be one way to support a “Boshin loyalty matters for rank” story. But it falls apart when you re-baseline the threshold to the figure the rule actually used.

仙台藩 (Sendai, Date) — sōdaka ~620,000 koku (well into marquess territory), but received only count. Lebra read this as a Boshin penalty.
会津藩 → 斗南藩 (Aizu → Tonami) — pre-Boshin sōdaka ~230,000 koku, but the post-Boshin remnant received only viscount.

title: 23  Patterns in the 1884 rank assignments
text:
23.2 The Asami (1994) correction
But Asami (1994) argues the threshold rule operated on genmai (現米) — the post-1869 tax-reported rice flow, which is much lower than sōdaka for most domains.
Re-checking:

Sendai genmai = 67,740 koku → squarely in count range. Sendai’s rank is mechanical, not punishment.
斗南 genmai = 7,380 koku → squarely in viscount range. Also mechanical.

(The post-Boshin territorial reduction of Aizu from 23万石 to 3万石 was itself the punishment — at the territorial level, not the rank level.)






NoteHow Sendai-Date appears in the 1884 register





In the Kanpō 第307号 peerage register, the Date house is conferred count rank under the standard lineage formula — 「授伯爵　從五位　伊達 宗基」 — the same wording used for the non-penalized houses around it. Sendai had been a pro-bakufu Northern Alliance leader and had its territory cut by more than half, yet the register treats the house no differently. This is consistent with the genmai-mechanical reading: the count rank was the threshold-rule outcome, not a discretionary penalty applied at the rank level. The broader point it illustrates is that the 1884 settlement accommodated the Restoration’s losing houses rather than writing them out.



Applying the genmai rule mechanically: of the 81 “kokudaka (石高) rule” deviations, 62 disappear (they were mechanical genmai outcomes all along). Only 19 remain — the truly anomalous cases.

title: 23  Patterns in the 1884 rank assignments
section: 23.7 Why this is a clean paper finding
text:
23.7 Why this is a clean paper finding
The simple conditional probability P(deviation | Boshin alignment) is statistically uninformative — roughly equal across groups (6.4% anti-bakufu vs 7.5% pro-bakufu). The naive marginal effect of Boshin alignment on “any deviation” is small.
But the structure of deviations is highly informative:

All 8 Restoration-service upgrades → anti-bakufu side
All 3 pro-bakufu upgrades → Tokugawa-lineage provisions (main-line, gosanke, Ōgyū-Matsudaira)
All 8 viscount→baron downgrades → former tsukegarō (付家老) or hatamoto (旗本) houses (orthogonal to Boshin)

The paper’s core empirical claim is therefore narrow but strong: the 1884 peerage system rewarded Restoration-service families specifically; the rule-bound mechanism of those rewards is identifiable in the data.

23.9 The 19, one by one
This section walks each of the 19 cases in turn, organised by deviation type. For families already covered in earlier walkthroughs — Shimazu (chapter 02b), Mōri (chapter 02c), and the five viscount→count houses (chapter 02i) — this section gives a paragraph summary with a cross-link. For the remaining 12 cases (徳川家達, 徳川篤敬, 松平茂昭, 伊達宗徳, and all eight viscount→baron houses), this section provides first-pass per-family narratives.






TipReading aid



The same family-not-domain framing from the top of this chapter applies throughout. Where a block says “Mōri received prince,” it means the Mōri family received prince rank in 1884, indexed to the domain (Chōshū) they ruled in 1870. Domains were abolished in 1871.



23.9.1 A. Marquess → Prince (3 cases)
The three prince rank assignments span the full Boshin spectrum: two anti-bakufu Restoration leaders (Shimazu of Satsuma, Mōri of Chōshū) and the Tokugawa main-line exception (Tokugawa Iesato, post-1868 head of the Sunpu/Shizuoka relocation).

699,182 koku (post-1868 relocation; pre-1868 shogunal tenryō was vastly larger)


Genmai
209,990 koku


Genmai-rule expectation
marquess


1884 outcome
prince (公爵)


Boshin role
Pro-bakufu (this is the former bakufu)


Why prince
Tokugawa main-line exception — its own clause, distinct from the gosanke and Restoration-leader clauses



After the bakufu’s defeat in 1868 the Tokugawa main line was relocated from Edo to a 70-万-石 territory centred on Sunpu (modern Shizuoka). The defeated party was nevertheless granted prince rank in 1884 because the kazoku-rei contained an explicit Tokugawa-main-line clause — separate from both the sanke clause (covering Owari/Kii/Mito) and the Restoration-leader clause (covering Shimazu/Mōri). 徳川家達 (1863–1940), great-grandson of the 11th shōgun (将軍) Ienari and grandson by adoption to the 14th shōgun Iemochi, was the recipient.
The methodological point: the 1884 rules ennobled by what the rules said, not by who won the war. The defeated central party received the highest rank — because the rules said so.



23.9.2 B. Count → Marquess (3 cases)
Three families received marquess rather than the genmai-predicted count. One was the explicit gosanke clause (Mito-Tokugawa); the other two were Restoration-loyalty rewards arriving years after the initial 1884 ennoblements (1888 for Echizen-Matsudaira/Fukui, 1891 for Date-Uwajima).

23.9.2.1 水戸藩 — Mito-Tokugawa, the gosanke clause







Family
水戸徳川家 (one of the gosanke (御三家))


1884 recipient
徳川篤敬


kokudaka (Hansei Ichiran gōdaka)
306,776 koku


Genmai
58,553 koku


Genmai-rule expectation
count


1884 outcome
marquess (侯爵)


Boshin role
Pro-bakufu by family-affiliation (the family produced the 15th shōgun Yoshinobu; Mito’s internal politics were violently divided)


Why marquess
Gosanke clause — explicit rule provision for the three Tokugawa cadet houses



Mito’s genmai of 58,553 koku sits squarely in count range. The 1884 kazoku-rei nevertheless elevated the family to marquess because of an explicit “徳川旧三家” clause: the heads of the three gosanke (御三家: Owari, Kii, Mito) were granted marquess rank regardless of genmai. Asami (1994), pp. 109–110, reproduces the rule text.
The clause was written for all three gosanke; Owari and Kii’s genmai were high enough to qualify for marquess on the standard rule, so only Mito triggered the actual exception. (Mito’s revenue had been substantially reduced through the 1860s; Owari and Kii were less affected.) Like Shizuoka, this is a case where the deviation is explained by a separately-written rule provision, not by Boshin alignment.


23.9.2.2 福井藩 — Echizen-Matsudaira, 1888 Restoration reward







Family
越前松平家 (Fukui line; founder 結城秀康 was Ieyasu’s second son)

1884 outcome
count → marquess (侯爵), 1888


Boshin role
Anti-bakufu (joined Restoration coalition through 公議政体派 channels)


Why upgraded
Restoration role — 松平春嶽 (=慶永) was a 四賢侯 / 公議政体派 figure central to the Restoration negotiations



Initially ennobled count in 1884 — exactly the genmai rule’s prediction — the Fukui-Matsudaira family was promoted to marquess in 1888. The citation given was the role of the previous head 松平春嶽 (松平慶永, 1828–1890) in the Meiji Restoration. Shungaku led the public-deliberation reformist faction within the broader Tokugawa orbit, served briefly as the bakufu’s 政事総裁職 in 1862, brokered between bakufu and court during the 1864–1867 negotiations, and joined the new government’s senior advisory ranks in 1868–1872.
The 1888 timing matters: it shows the 1884 cohort was not the only ennoblement event. The Meiji peerage system allowed promotion for Restoration service on a rolling basis as historical assessments matured. Fukui is the canonical example of this rolling-promotion mechanism.


23.9.2.3 宇和島藩 — Date-Uwajima, 1891 Restoration reward

Boshin role
Anti-bakufu — 伊達宗城 (Munenari) was a 四賢侯 figure who joined the Restoration faction


Why upgraded
Restoration role — Munenari’s status as a 公議政体派 figure and Restoration-government advisor



Uwajima received count rank in 1884 — exactly what the genmai rule predicts. In 1891 the family was promoted to marquess, citing the role of 伊達宗城 (1818–1892), one of the 四賢侯 (Four Wise Lords) of late-Tokugawa Japan alongside Shungaku of Fukui, 山内容堂 of Tosa, and 島津斉彬 of Satsuma. Like Shungaku, Munenari was a bridging figure during the 1860s court-bakufu negotiations and served in senior advisory roles in the early Meiji government.
The structural irony: the main Date line at Sendai received only count rank (because their post-Boshin genmai of 67,740 koku fell into the count band — itself partly a Boshin-induced territorial reduction), while the Date cadet at Uwajima was promoted past them to marquess. The Date name appears on both sides of the Restoration ledger.



23.9.3 C. Viscount → Count (5 cases — see chapter 02i for full narratives)
The five viscount→count upgrades are families with genmai well below 50,000 koku who received count rank anyway. Four of the five are on the anti-bakufu / pro-restoration side; the fifth, Tatsuoka (龍岡), is a pro-bakufu Tokugawa-lineage house (Ōgyū-Matsudaira), with 大給恒’s personal post-1868 service noted as part of its individual record.

23.9.4 D. Viscount → Baron (8 cases — post-1868 newly-elevated daimyō)
All eight viscount→baron downgrades share a single structural feature: each family became “true daimyō” only at the end of the Tokugawa era or at the Restoration itself. Either they were 付家老 (vassals-of-a-vassal, attached to a major-domain house) detached at the 1868 reorganisation, or they were 旗本 (direct Tokugawa banner-men) bumped above the 10,000-koku daimyō threshold in 1868–1869.
The 1884 kazoku-rei contained a separate provision for these post-1868 elevations: rather than receiving viscount as the genmai rule would predict, they were placed at baron, reflecting their pre-Restoration baishin / hatamoto status. The downgrade is therefore explained by pre-1868 institutional history, not by Boshin alignment — seven of the eight are coded anti-bakufu and one is neutral (Nariwa); none is pro-bakufu. The pattern is orthogonal to Restoration coalition.
The eight split 4 + 4 between the two routes.
The 4 付家老 (vassal-of-a-vassal) cases:

23.9.4.1 今尾藩 — 竹腰家, Owari 付家老

Why baron
付家老 — Owari attached house, only independent post-1868



The Takenokoshi were one of three 付家老 houses attached to Owari-Tokugawa (along with Ishikawa and Naruse). Their function for centuries was to serve as senior advisors and de-facto overseers of the Owari main line on the bakufu’s behalf — a structural device by which the bakufu kept direct visibility into the major cadet branches. The Takenokoshi held about 30,000 koku in Mino (around Imao, modern Gifu) but counted as Owari baishin rather than independent daimyō. In 1868 the 付家老 system was dissolved and the three Owari attached houses became formally independent. The 1884 baron rank reflects this very recent independence.


23.9.4.2 新宮藩 — 水野家, Kii 付家老

Of the Kii (紀州) attached structure, two 付家老 houses — Mizuno of 新宮 (eastern Kii) and Andō of 田辺 (western Kii) — were detached at the Restoration. The Mizuno had administered eastern Kii from Shingū for over two centuries while formally remaining Kii baishin. Their post-1868 independence + baron rank follows the same pattern as the Owari 付家老.

The Nakayama were Mito’s 付家老 — the structural parallel to Takenokoshi in Owari. Their seat at Matsuoka (modern Ibaraki) was small, but they had long held senior administrative roles within the Mito han, including arbiter functions during Mito’s violently divided 1864–1868 internal politics (the 天狗党 / 諸生党 conflict). The Hondō were a Hitachi (modern Ibaraki) 旗本 (hatamoto) house at 8,000 koku for most of the Tokugawa era — just below the 10,000-koku daimyō threshold the bakufu used to distinguish daimyō from banner-men. In 1868 the new imperial government bumped them to 10,000 koku, making them nominally daimyō. Their 1884 baron rank reflects this very recent elevation; they had never been “real” multigenerational daimyō of the kind the standard viscount-tier rule envisioned.

The Yamasaki of Nariwa (modern Okayama, 高梁市) were 交代寄合 (kōtai yoriai) — an intermediate status between 旗本 and full daimyō that gave the family some daimyō-like privileges (sankin kōtai attendance) without being formally counted as daimyō. They held about 5,000 koku for most of the Tokugawa era and were promoted to 10,000 koku daimyō status in 1868. Their baron rank follows the same post-1868 elevation rule.
Nariwa is the lone “neutral” in this 8-case group — neither anti- nor pro-bakufu in the Boshin classification. This is consistent with the deeper claim that the viscount→baron downgrades are about pre-Restoration institutional status rather than Boshin alignment.

23.9.4.8 矢島藩 — 生駒家, late 旗本 elevation with active Boshin role







Family

Boshin role
Anti-bakufu — Northern Alliance member who defected to the imperial side during the war


Why baron
旗本-equivalent → 1万石 elevation only in 1869



The most institutionally interesting case in this 8-group. The Ikoma had been daimyō of Sanuki-Takamatsu (讃岐高松藩, ~17 万石) until the 1640 御家騒動 (succession scandal), after which the main domain was confiscated and the family reduced to a small Dewa (modern Akita) fief at 8,000 koku — 旗本-equivalent status. They held this position for over two centuries.
In the Boshin War (Boshin sensō) the Ikoma joined the Northern Alliance (奥羽越列藩同盟) but defected to the imperial side mid-war — one of the Northern Alliance defectors covered in chapter 02g. The new government rewarded the defection by elevating them back to 10,000-koku daimyō status in 1869. Despite this active anti-bakufu service, however, the 1884 kazoku-rei still placed them at baron under the post-1868-elevation provision. The Ikoma case is therefore especially diagnostic: it shows the baron downgrade was structural (about pre-Restoration daimyō status) rather than discretionary (Boshin reward or punishment).

Methodological hook: 浅見雅男『華族誕生 名誉と体面の明治 (Kazoku tanjō: meiyo to taimen no Meiji, “Birth of the Peerage: Honor and Reputation in Meiji”)』リブロポート (1994), pp. 87–88 explicitly articulates the genmai threshold rule. Lebra (1993) uses the kokudaka framing. The dataset stores both deviation values side by side — the kokudaka-based (Lebra) variant and the genmai-based (Asami) variant — so the paper can present whichever baseline the analysis calls for.
crumbs: 23  Patterns in the 1884 rank assignments

title: 24  Sources and citations
text:
24.1 The three source families
This chapter documents the citation chain behind every variable in the analytic dataset: what each source is, what variables it backs, and the form in which it should be cited.
The dataset draws on three structurally different families of sources, each with its own role and citation pattern.

title: 24  Sources and citations
text:
24.1.1 Family 1 — Hansei Ichiran 1870 (the primary record)
The book itself: 藩制一覧 (Hansei Ichiran), 1870, two volumes, surveyed and compiled by the early Meiji government. Every hi_* column in the analytic dataset derives from this source, traced through per-domain extracted chunks.
Citation form: Hansei Ichiran (藩制一覧), 1870, vol. N, table N, page N. Compiled by the Meiji-government survey of daimyō* (大名) administrative returns.*
The exact page reference for any Hansei Ichiran-derived value is preserved in the dataset’s extraction record (one row per per-domain per-field extraction, with the source page noted). When a paper table cites a specific Hansei Ichiran number, this row-level provenance establishes precisely where in the original document the value lives.
Volume contents:

Volume 1: each domain’s fiscal and demographic data — kokudaka (石高) in its several variants, tax receipts, population, household counts.
Volume 2: each domain’s military and administrative data — troop strength, warships, officer rosters. Hansei Ichiran does not record warships for Saga (佐嘉藩); the dataset draws Saga’s navy from 藤野保 (2010).



24.1.2 Family 2 — Government primary documents (paper-citable)
Three contemporaneous government compilations that overlap with Hansei Ichiran on population and revenue, plus the official ennoblement gazettes:

24.1.2.1 明治史要 (Meiji Shiyō)
Compiled by the 修史局 (Shūshikyoku, “Bureau of Historiography”). The standard early-Meiji compendium of administrative data: domain populations, household counts, kokudaka, basic fiscal indicators. Backs the population_meiji_shiyo and households_meiji_shiyo columns.
Citation form: 明治史要 (Meiji Shiyō), Bureau of Historiography (修史局), early Meiji compendium.


24.1.2.2 大隈重信関係資料 (Ōkuma Shigenobu kankei shiryō, “Ōkuma Shigenobu Papers”)「府藩県石高人口表 (Fu-han-ken kokudaka jinkō-hyō, “Prefecture-Domain-County Kokudaka and Population Table”)」
Held in the Ōkuma Shigenobu papers (大隈重信関係資料) at the Waseda University Library, call number イ14-A4361. The “府藩県石高人口表” is a contemporary tabulation of administrative-unit kokudaka and population. Backs the population_okuma and uchidaka_okuma_koku columns.
📄 Download the scanned manuscript (1.7 MB PDF) — Waseda University Library digital archive, freely accessible.
Citation form: 「府藩県石高人口表」, 大隈重信関係資料, Ōkuma Shigenobu papers, Waseda University Library archival collection (call number イ14-A4361).


24.1.2.3 旧高旧領取調帳 (Kyūdaka kyūryō torishirabechō, “Register of Former Kokudaka and Former Fief-Holders”), 1872 Meiji cadastral survey
The early Meiji government’s per-village cadastral survey, compiled 1872 from the village-level land-yield rolls (kokudaka) that the daimyō domains had been administering pre-haihan-chiken. The “旧” (“former”) in the title refers to the pre-1869 Edo-period feudal arrangement: each village entry records (a) its old province (旧国), district (旧郡), village (旧村), and lord (旧領); (b) its rated kokudaka (旧高); and (c) the 1872 Meiji prefecture (旧県) administering it post-haihan.
Hosted as a free open-access searchable database by the National Museum of Japanese History (国立歴史民俗博物館 / Rekihaku):
📄 旧高旧領取調帳データベース (Rekihaku) — freely accessible; query by 旧領名 / 旧国 / 旧郡 / 旧村.
Backs the rekihaku_kyudaka_iv_koku column, which feeds the composite kokudaka_iv as Tier 4 for the 13 domains absent from HI vol 1’s kokudaka coverage (仙台, 新発田, 杵築, 母里, 清崎, 足利, 須坂, 岩村, 対馬府中, 岩国, 岩崎, 松岡, 斗南). The Rekihaku 旧高 is a kusadaka-tier metric (rated land yield from village rolls) — the same conceptual tier as HI 草高 / 合高 — giving methodological consistency to the IV across all 286 rows.
For 仙台藩 specifically the IV uses only the post-Boshin retained territory (仙台県 + 宮城県 + distant 常陸/下総 hatamoto enclaves) = 299,659 koku, EXCLUDING the four 1869 carve-off prefectures (登米県 + 胆沢県 + 江刺県 + 角田県 = 674,350 koku) that were no longer Sendai-Date revenue at the moment of haihan-chiken.
Citation form: 旧高旧領取調帳 (1872 Meiji cadastral survey), per 国立歴史民俗博物館 旧高旧領取調帳データベース (https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param), accessed 2026-05-24.


24.1.2.4 統計集誌 (Tōkei Shūshi, “Statistical Journal”) “人員表 (Jin’in-hyō,”Personnel Table”)”
Compiled by 呉文聡 from 野村文夫’s materials, published in Tōkei Shūshi (the early-Meiji statistical journal). Backs the population_tokei_shushi column.
Citation form: 統計集誌 (Tōkei Shūshi), 人員表, compiled by 呉文聡 from 野村文夫 materials.


24.1.2.5 官報 (Kanpō, “Official Gazette”) 第307号 (1884-07-08) — primary ennoblement gazette (daimyō / sekke (摂家) cohort)
The government gazette that recorded the 1884-07-07 ennoblement decree — the original 公侯伯子男 (prince / marquess / count / viscount / baron) rank assignments. This is the primary record underlying every kazoku_rank_actual value, with a small number of late-cohort exceptions handled by Kanpō #308.
📄 View the gazette (NDL Digital Collections, pid 2943511, 9 pages) — freely accessible, Public Domain Mark.
Citation form: 官報 第307号 (1884-07-08), NDL pid 2943511.


24.1.2.6 官報 第308号 (1884-07-09) — primary ennoblement gazette (late cohort)
The follow-up gazette for daimyō whose rank assignments lagged the main decree by one day. Backs a small number of kazoku_rank_actual rows including 半原藩 and several other edge cases.
📄 View the gazette (NDL Digital Collections, pid 2943512, 9 pages) — freely accessible, Public Domain Mark.
Citation form: 官報 第308号 (1884-07-09), NDL Digital Collections (pid 2943512).



24.1.3 Family 3 — Scholarly synthesis (paper-citable)
Secondary scholarly works treated not as the original record but as the synthesis-and-interpretation layer between the primary documents and the dataset’s coded values.

24.1.3.1 浅見雅男『華族誕生 名誉と体面の明治 (Kazoku tanjō: meiyo to taimen no Meiji, “Birth of the Peerage: Honor and Reputation in Meiji”)』(リブロポート 1994)
The single most-important scholarly source for the dataset’s central kazoku (華族)-rank variables. Asami’s work:

Reconstructs the 1884 kazoku-rei threshold rule with sufficient detail that the genmai (現米)-based mechanical-expected rank (kazoku_rank_expected_genmai) can be computed for every domain.
Establishes genmai — not sōdaka (草高) — as the threshold numerator. Pre-Asami sources (including Lebra 1993) implicitly used kokudaka as the threshold; Asami shows the rule explicitly operated on post-1869 genmai. This single point reduces the apparent “Boshin penalty” sample from 93 cases (Lebra framing) to 19 (Asami framing) by mechanically explaining 76 outcomes. The Sendai and Aizu cases that look like Restoration punishments turn out to be genmai-mechanical (see chapter 07).
Documents the explicit exception clauses (gosanke clause, Tokugawa-main-line clause, Restoration-leader clause, sankyō (御三卿) clause, post-1868-elevation clause) — each of which explains a specific subset of deviations from the standard rule.

Pages 87–88 articulate the genmai rule; pages 109–110 give the gosanke and sankyō clauses; pages 105–115 the Restoration-leader exception. Asami is the citation in 94 percent of kazoku_rank_source rows.
Citation form: 浅見雅男『華族誕生 名誉と体面の明治』リブロポート (1994).


24.1.3.2 Beasley, W.G. The Meiji Restoration. Stanford University Press (1972)
The standard English-language synthesis of the Restoration period. Used for:

Domain classifications — Beasley p. xii lists “Principal Domains in 1860” with explicit shinpan (親藩) / fudai (譜代) / tozama designations; this is the cross-check used to confirm 仙台 (Date) and 対馬府中 (Sō) as tozama (外様).
Boshin alignment — Beasley ch. XI pp. 295–299 provides the standard chronology of Toba-Fushimi and the subsequent imperial consolidation. The bulk boshin_alignment_source entries for roughly 212 small viscount-tier domains cite Beasley.
Hikone defection — Beasley p. 297 is the only English-language source that explicitly names Hikone as the pre-Toba-Fushimi defector (the Ii family broke with the bakufu (幕府) after Ii Naosuke’s assassination in 1860 and the subsequent Sakuradamon Incident).

Citation form: Beasley, W. G. The Meiji Restoration. Stanford University Press (1972).


24.1.3.3 Esposito, Gabriele. Japanese Armies 1868–1877: The Boshin War and Satsuma Rebellion. Osprey Men-at-Arms 530 (2020)
A military-history reference with detailed coverage of the Boshin War (戊辰戦争) combatants. Used for Boshin alignment citations on the major-domain combatants (the focal four plus the Northern Alliance core: Sendai, Yonezawa, Shōnai, Nagaoka, Nihonmatsu, Morioka, Kuwana). Backs nineteen boshin_alignment_source rows.
The volume is in the project workspace. Page guide for Boshin-alignment claims:

pp. 5–7 — Conflicts with Chōshū 1863–66 + European military missions
pp. 8–11 — Boshin War narrative: Operations January–July 1868 (Toba-Fushimi, Sat-Chō-Tosa imperial alliance), Northern Alliance May–October 1868, Republic of Ezo October 1868–June 1869
p. 22 — South-western forces (Satsuma + Chōshū modernisation)
pp. 23–28 — Shogunate forces, Denshutai, police, Aizu forces, Hokkaido 1869
pp. 32–33 — Chōshū forces (Kiheitai etc.)

Citation form: Esposito, Gabriele. Japanese Armies 1868–1877: The Boshin War and Satsuma Rebellion. Osprey Men-at-Arms 530, Osprey Publishing (2020), specific pages as cited.


24.1.3.4 工藤威『奥羽列藩同盟の基礎的研究 (Ōu reppan dōmei no kisoteki kenkyū, “Basic Research on the Northern Alliance”)』(岩田書院 2002)
The standard Japanese-language academic monograph on the 奥羽越列藩同盟 (Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei) — the Northern Alliance of bakumatsu domains that resisted the imperial advance after Toba-Fushimi. Backs the Boshin alignment citations for the twelve Northern Alliance combatants plus the nine Northern Alliance defectors who switched sides mid-war, and supplied the corrections to several Northern Alliance roster mis-classifications.
The monograph is not in the project workspace and would need to be acquired via interlibrary loan. The roster (the load-bearing content for this dataset) is transcribed in the JP Wikipedia article on 奥羽越列藩同盟, which footnotes back to Kudō’s specific pages — the dataset cites Kudō via the Wikipedia transcription with that as the verification chain.
Citation form: 工藤威『奥羽列藩同盟の基礎的研究』岩田書院 (2002), roster transcribed in JP Wikipedia 奥羽越列藩同盟.


24.1.3.5 児玉幸多 ed. 『藩史総覧 (Hanshi sōran, “Comprehensive Survey of Han History”)』新人物往来社 (1977)
The canonical Japanese-language han-classification compendium. The principal source for domain_category (shinpan / fudai / tozama / sankyō / tenryō (天領)). Establishes formal category designations for all roughly 300 domains.
Citation form: 児玉幸多 編『藩史総覧』新人物往来社 (1977).


24.1.3.6 国史大辞典 (Kokushi daijiten, “Great Dictionary of National History”) (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1979–1997)
The standard Japanese-language reference dictionary. Used as the secondary verification layer for individual house histories, daimyō biographies, and domain administrative changes. Not typically cited per row, but listed in the paper bibliography as the underlying reference framework.
Citation form: 国史大辞典 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1979–1997 edition).


24.1.3.7 華族大鑑 (Kazoku taikan, “Compendium of the Peerage”)刊行会 編『華族大鑑』(1990)
The dedicated reference for the 1884 prince-rank (公爵) ennoblements specifically — used for the three prince cases in the dataset (Shimazu / Satsuma, Mōri / Chōshū, Tokugawa / Shizuoka) and as a supplementary citation alongside Asami for several marquess cases.
Citation form: 華族大鑑刊行会 編『華族大鑑』(1990).


24.1.3.8 小田部雄次『華族 近代日本貴族の虚像と実像 (Kazoku: kindai Nihon kizoku no kyozō to jitsuzō, “The Peerage: Image and Reality of the Modern Japanese Aristocracy”)』中公新書 (2006)
A modern general-reader-facing book on the kazoku system as a whole. Used for several small viscount-tier kazoku_rank_source rows where the family didn’t appear in Asami’s main tables.
Citation form: 小田部雄次『華族 近代日本貴族の虚像と実像』中公新書 (2006).


24.1.3.9 松田敬之『〈華族爵位〉請願人名辞典 (Kazoku shakui seigan jinmei jiten, “Dictionary of Peerage-Title Petitioners”)』吉川弘文館 (2015)
Definitive on the 1884 exclusions — daimyō families that were not ennobled because of the kaieki (改易, confiscation) or similar disqualifying events. Backs the documented exclusion of 堀江藩 (大沢家, 万石事件 1871) and the verification of 半原藩.
Citation form: 松田敬之『〈華族爵位〉請願人名辞典』吉川弘文館 (2015).


24.1.3.10 Mark Ravina. Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan. Stanford University Press (1999)
Provides the dataset’s ratio variables (kusadaka/commoner, retainers/100 commoners, gendaka (現高)/commoner, retainers/1000 koku, gendaka/kusadaka ratio) from Appendix Table A1 of the book. 209 domains in the dataset have Ravina coverage. One confirmed typo: Ravina A1’s value for Akita 久保田藩 gendaka_per_commoner is 0.049 but the primary-derived value is 0.49 (a decimal-place error). The dataset uses 0.49 (the corrected Ravina value) or the primary-derived 0.457, and flags the discrepancy explicitly.
Citation form: Mark Ravina. Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan. Stanford University Press (1999), Appendix Table A1.


24.1.3.11 Focal-domain published-source supplements
For each of the four focal domains (Saga, Satsuma, Chōshū, Tosa) a published-source supplement lists citable references for military, political, and institutional details that go beyond what Hansei Ichiran provides:

Saga supplement — 藤野保『佐賀藩』(2010); Jentschura 1977 (warships); Beasley 1972
Satsuma supplement — Esposito 2020; Jentschura 1977; Beasley 1972; Craig 1961/2000
Chōshū supplement — Craig 1961/2000; Jansen 1961/1994
Tosa supplement — Roberts 1998; Jansen 1961/1994

Each supplement provides a citation-ready reference block for that focal domain’s narrative section.

The bulk-citation pattern (the 212 anti-bakufu, 19 Tokugawa-affiliate, and 10 neutral domains) uses a single Beasley-1972-chapter-XI reference that frames the standard chronology rather than enumerating each domain individually. Beasley’s analysis is that after Toba-Fushimi the imperial side issued a general directive and most small domains complied without taking an active combatant role. The dataset follows this — coding them anti-bakufu by end-state rather than by Boshin participation.

title: 24  Sources and citations
section: 24.5 Materials used as finders only — never cited
text:
24.5 Materials used as finders only — never cited
Two ancillary materials are retained for internal validation but never appear as citations.

24.5.1 Japanese Wikipedia (and English Wikipedia)
Used as a finder to:

Locate primary-source candidates (when a Wikipedia article cites Meiji Shiyō or 浅見 1994, those underlying sources get verified and cited).
Cross-validate values that already have a citable source (when Asami 1994 says marquess and the Japanese Wikipedia article on the family also says marquess, that confirms transcription).
Seed initial values for last_lord and provincial-location fields. Those fields are kept only where they have an independent primary citation.

Wikipedia is never the citation; the underlying primary source it references is.


24.5.2 Bakumatsu-era domain-listing website
Used as an internal cross-validation layer for:

domain_category initial coding (now fully superseded by 児玉 1977 + Beasley 1972 re-citation).
boshin_alignment_group initial coding (now fully superseded by the per-row citation chain documented above).
last_lord and provincial fields (retained as cross-checks; would require independent re-citation before any paper inclusion).

This source is never cited. It was retained only because the initial bulk-data scrape was the spine that allowed the dataset to bootstrap. Every paper-relevant field has since been re-cited to an academic source.

title: 25  Interactive map (prototype)
text:
25.1 What the map can do
Each polygon is a daimyō (大名) domain (藩) from 1870. You can toggle what the colours mean using the layer control in the top-right of the map.
The map is a single self-contained HTML file — it works offline, needs no server, and embeds in this walkthrough as readily as it opens on its own. It offers:
Every value in a popup is the same data as the static choropleth maps elsewhere in the walkthrough — the interactive map simply lets you query it domain by domain.

title: 25  Interactive map (prototype)
text:
Five colour layers — 1884 kazoku rank, kokudaka, Boshin alignment, Tokugawa-era category, and the 19 genmai-rule deviations — switched from the layer control in the top-right corner.
A jump-to-domain search box (top-left) — type a domain in romanized or kanji form to fly straight to it.
An EN / 日本語 toggle — switches every popup and the legend between English and Japanese.
Click-through data cards — every one of the 286 domains opens a full card: the ruling family, the 1884 rank recipient, revenue, demographics, military, Boshin alignment, the rank-deviation diagnostic, and a per-row source list. The four 薩長土肥 focal domains also link out to their walkthrough chapters and key scholarly works.



Implementation: Folium 0.20 (a Python wrapper for Leaflet.js). Polygon source: Harvard CHGIS 1664 daimyō shapefile. Data: analytic_wide.csv (286 × 130). Output: a self-contained HTML map (interactive_map.html).

text:
26.2 Sample scope
The 9 pages cover the four focal domains plus two pedagogically-important cases — Satsuma’s military-table 書ナラス non-disclosure and Saga’s Table-9 army. Each page demonstrates a different HI structural pattern:








Page

The kokudaka aggregates: 草高 869k, the breakdown into 損高 / 高 / 現石, the famous Ryūkyū sugar monopoly figure (5.09M jin / ~3,055 tons/year).

The Table 8 page with the literal annotation 書ナラス (“not specified”) under the domain header — explicit documentation of Satsuma’s refusal to disclose military structure.


3

Demographics, including the 内 X 人口 sub-row pattern (11,589 shizoku + 10,362 sotsu = 22k samurai-class out of 66k total population — a notably high samurai share).


4

The kokudaka components: 草高 369,411 + 外高 618,593 + 合高 988,004 — the figures add up exactly.


5
