The 5-year-average kokudaka (495k, covering 1864-1868 = the Boshin-precursor years) and a long product list characteristic of large domains.


6
土佐 (Tosa)
Shizoku/sotsu population detail (30,506 shizoku + 17,681 sotsu). Also the 穢多戸數 row — a pre-1871-emancipation outcast-class count.


7
佐賀 (Saga)
The kokudaka entry using Saga’s distinctive 貮口合 平均 (“two-mouth combined, average”) phrasing for what other domains call 合高. Saga has no vol1 entry — only vol2 — so this is its only kokudaka page.


8
佐賀 (Saga)
Demographics (23,823 shizoku + 43,488 sotsu — sotsu is nearly double shizoku, an unusual ratio) followed by the start of ○佐倉藩 (Sakura-han, a different domain) on the same page — illustrating HI’s stacked-page pattern.


9
佐賀 (Saga)
Saga’s Table-9 army. Six battalions, 2,972 troops (officers 864 + artillery-soldiers 180 + infantry 1,440 + artillery-privates 108 + infantry-privates 260 + bandsmen 120) and 36 cannon. It prints on the 佐倉 (Sakura) page and reads right-to-left up to the ○佐倉藩 marker; Table 9 writes the domain with the modern 佐賀 rather than the old-kanji 佐嘉 used elsewhere, which makes the entry easy to overlook.

title: 26  HI page viewer (focal-4 sample)
text:
26.3 Why this design
Tying a tabular dataset back to its source normally means opening the original page, finding the value, and comparing it by hand — slow for one cell, prohibitive for a 7,000-row dataset. The viewer collapses that to a single hover: the annotation points directly at the value on the page, shows the verbatim kanji, and links it to its row in the dataset. Every number is one motion away from the brushed original it came from.
The pedagogical layer (modern Japanese reading + English translation) makes HI accessible to readers who don’t read kanbun-style Meiji-era government prose. The 1870 administrative kanji is technically modern Japanese but uses kyūjitai (旧字体, pre-1946 character forms), unfamiliar archaic vocabulary (sōdaka, gendaka, sotsu-zoku, etc.), and Edo-period unit names — all of which the per-annotation translation explicitly disambiguates.

title: 26  HI page viewer (focal-4 sample)
text:
26.4 What’s next
If this 9-page sample is useful, the methodology scales to all 286 domains. The bottleneck is manual annotation — about ~10 minutes per page to identify bounding boxes and write the trilingual triplets. A full coverage of all 286 domains would be roughly 500-600 pages and 60-100 hours of annotation work. Worth considering as a follow-on project if the format proves valuable for verification or for pedagogical exhibition of the source.

Image source: Hansei Ichiran (藩制一覧, 1870 — Ministry of Civil Affairs survey of domain institutions). High-resolution page scans extracted from the workspace-resident PDFs at Primary_Sources/Hansei Ichiran (1870) - Survey of Domain Institutions Vol [1/2].pdf.
Annotation methodology: Bounding boxes hand-drawn against each page; verbatim kanji transcribed character-by-character; modern Japanese reading and English translation written with reference to standard kokudaka / status-class vocabulary (Furushima 1968 on Edo fiscal terminology; Lebra 1993 on kazoku status conventions; Yamamura 1974 on samurai stipend categories).
Source data: Each annotation links to the corresponding row in the project’s transcription of Hansei Ichiran; page-level extracts are pre-computed for the viewer.
Viewer technology: OpenSeadragon 4.1.0 for deep-zoom pan/zoom of the page, with custom CSS overlays positioned in image coordinates and a kanji / modern-Japanese / English annotation toggle. Annotation bounding boxes use the W3C xywh=pixel Web-Annotation fragment format.

title: 27  Primary sources
text:
27.1 The documents
The chapters in this book quote a handful of contemporary documents — an imperial decree, the diaries and accounts of the men who carried the reform out, and the laws that settled it. This section gathers those documents in one place, each on its own page: the original Japanese as it appears in the source, an English working translation, and a short note on what the document shows.
These are the records the 1871 Abolition narrative is built from. Where the narrative quotes a line, you can read it here in full and in context.

NoteA note on the texts



The Japanese is transcribed from the original scans (mostly held at the National Diet Library) and read character by character; uncertain readings are not presented as certain. The English is a working translation meant to convey the sense for readers who do not read Meiji-era administrative Japanese — it is not a literary rendering. Each page gives the exact source and page reference so any quotation can be checked against the original.

title: 28  The abolition decree
text:
28.1 The proclamation
Meiji 4 / 7 / 14 — 29 August 1871
On 29 August 1871 the emperor abolished the domains (haihan chiken 廃藩置県). Two contemporary records preserve the moment: the imperial proclamation itself, reproduced in Inoue Kaoru’s biography, and the government’s own chronicle, Meiji Shiyō, which records who was summoned and commended that day.
The text of the imperial rescript, as reproduced in the Inoue biography:
The two reasons the decree gives for itself — order at home, standing abroad — are the frame the whole reform was justified in: not punishment of the domains, but a state made coherent enough to survive in a dangerous world.

title: 28  The abolition decree
text:
朕惟フニ、更始ノ時ニ際シ、内以テ億兆ヲ保安シ、外以テ萬國ト對峙セント欲セハ、宜ク名實相副ヒ、政令一ニ歸セシムヘシ。朕嚢ニ諸藩版籍奉還ノ議ヲ聴納シ、新ニ知藩事ヲ命シ、各其職ヲ奉セシム。然ルニ數百年因襲ノ久キ、或ハ其名アリテ其實舉ラサル者アリ。… 仍テ今更ニ藩ヲ廢シ縣ト爲ス。… 汝群臣其レ朕カ意ヲ體セヨ。


On reflection: at this moment of new beginning — if we are to safeguard the millions at home and stand on a par with the nations of the world abroad — name and substance must correspond, and political authority must be unified in one place. I previously heeded the proposal of the various domains for the return of the registers, newly appointed governors, and had each take up that office. But after several hundred years of inherited custom, some hold the name without the substance. … Therefore I now abolish the domains and make them prefectures. … You, my ministers, embody my intent.

title: 28  The abolition decree
text:
28.2 The chronicle entry
Meiji Shiyō’s entry for the same day records the ceremony and the commendations:

十四日詔シテ列藩ヲ廢シテ悉ク縣ト爲ス。… 各藩知事ヲ御前ニ召シ、勅シテ島津忠義、毛利元徳、鍋島直大、山内豊範ノ版籍奉還ヲ首唱セシヲ賞シ、又徳川慶勝、細川護久、池田慶徳、蜂須賀茂韶ノ建議ヲ立纒シ、其當ヲ得テ嘉ス。


Day 14: by decree, the domains are abolished and all made into prefectures. … The domain governors were summoned before the emperor; by imperial word, Shimazu Tadayoshi [Satsuma], Mōri Motonori [Chōshū], Nabeshima Naohiro [Saga], and Yamauchi Toyonori [Tosa] were praised for having first proposed the return of the registers; and Tokugawa Yoshikatsu [Owari], Hosokawa Morihisa [Kumamoto], Ikeda Yoshinori [Tottori], and Hachisuka Mochiaki [Tokushima], whose proposals had been adopted, were commended with imperial approval.

title: 28  The abolition decree
text:
28.3 What it shows
The chronicle names eight commended houses, in two groups: the four leading domains of the Restoration — Satsuma, Chōshū, Saga, Tosa — credited for proposing the 1869 return of the registers, and four more credited for helping build the new system. The second group spans the status spectrum, from a senior Tokugawa-related house (Owari) to major outside lords (Kumamoto, Tokushima). The public thanks went to a wider circle than the famous four — one small sign of how broadly the bargain reached. The narrative of the day is in The bargain of 1871.

Proclamation text: 『世外井上公伝 (Sekkai Inoue-kō Den)』vol. 1, book p. 452 (the imperial rescript as reproduced in Inoue Kaoru’s authorized biography, 1933–34).
Chronicle entry: 修史局編『明治史要 (Meiji Shiyō)』本紀 vol. 1, Meiji 4/7/14 entry, book pp. 26–28. National Diet Library Digital Collections.

title: 29  Kido Takayoshi’s diary
text:
29.1 The secret conference — 27 August
27–31 August 1871 (Meiji 4 / 7 / 12–16)
Kido Takayoshi of Chōshū was, by his own and others’ accounts, the chief political architect of the 1871 abolition. His diary for the decree week is the closest first-person record of how it was done. The quotations below are from the standard English edition (Brown & Hirota, 1983).
Two days before the decree, the three core architects settled the plan and took it for ratification through two separate channels:

title: 29  Kido Takayoshi’s diary
text: 27 August 1871. … Saigō, Ōkubo, and I held a secret conference for starting to implement our plan. … Thereupon Saigō and I reported our plan to Prince Sanjō, asking him to report it to the Throne … Ōkubo and I gave an account of our plan to Lord Iwakura. Originally the argument was advanced that we should not tell Lord Iwakura beforehand. As Lord Iwakura has been connected with all important matters since the time of the Restoration, I was reluctant not to tell him about this.

title: 29  Kido Takayoshi’s diary
text:
29.2 The decree — 29 August

An Imperial message to our Lord Governor of Chōshū, and to the Lords Shimazu, Yamanouchi, and Nabeshima stated that the abolition of the domains would be proclaimed today. As the Governor of Nagoya, the Governors Ikeda, Hosokawa, and Hachisuka, have submitted petitions requesting the reform, there were Imperial messages especially for them. … At 2 the Emperor appeared in the Grand Hall … and the governors of fifty-six domains were summoned before him. There the Imperial edict on the abolition of the domains was read to them; and the governors were all dismissed from their offices.

title: 29  Kido Takayoshi’s diary
section: 29.3 How it was done — Kido’s retrospective
text:
29.3 How it was done — Kido’s retrospective
In the same entry, Kido looked back on the years of maneuver behind the decree — including the legal argument he used to make confiscation defensible, and the weaker compromise he had to fight off:

I contrived a stratagem by saying that the fiefs of the lords had been granted to them by the Tokugawa, an Imperial enemy. They had never been authorized under the Imperial seal; thus, I asked, how can the Empire stand unless it is made very clear that one’s highest loyalty is to the Emperor? For this reason I advocated the Return of the Registers to the Throne, persuading Satsuma first, then Tosa and Hizen …


Finally, the Imperial Government reached an agreement to appoint the feudal lords as hereditary governors of the domains. In consequence, I argued as strongly as possible against that arrangement, and finally we eliminated the two characters for ‘hereditary.’

He also recorded the personal danger: “many there were who advocated assassinating me … I was on the alert wherever I went.”

title: 29  Kido Takayoshi’s diary
text:
29.4 The Chōshū lord’s petition
The most quoted lines come at the end of the day, as the Chōshū lord Mōri Motonori — who had petitioned for the reform days earlier — prostrated himself among the dismissed governors:

a man to whom my obligations are deeper than the seas and higher than the mountains, prostrated himself as one of the fifty-six domain governors, and listened to the Imperial Edict. At this I choked up as my emotions welled up within me, and my tears fell in spite of myself. … For this purpose he submitted his petition to the Throne a few days ago. His act made me feel that I should make every effort to have it accepted; and I did as much as my limited strength would permit toward that end.

title: 29  Kido Takayoshi’s diary
text:
29.5 What it shows
The lord petitions; the architect works to get the petition accepted. Read together with the dual-channel ratification — where even the three architects had to seek formal approval through both Sanjō and Iwakura — the diary shows a reform that no one could simply impose: it had to be carried by petition, persuasion, and ratification at every step. The narrative is in The bargain of 1871.

Source: The Diary of Kido Takayoshi, vol. II (1871–1874), translated by Sidney DeVere Brown & Akiko Hirota (University of Tokyo Press, 1983), book pp. 63–67 (entries of 27–31 August 1871). The original is the 木戸孝允日記 (Kido Takayoshi Nikki).

title: 30  Inoue Kaoru’s account
text:
30.1 The fiscal case
From the 1933–34 authorized biography
Inoue Kaoru was deputy Finance Minister (Ōkura-tayū 大蔵大輔) in 1871 and one of the men who planned and carried out the abolition. His authorized biography devotes a chapter to it, drawing on his own papers and his later recollections. Three passages give the implementer’s view: why the reform was urgent, how it was planned, and how it would be enforced.
Asked what brought two colleagues to his door, Inoue answered that only one thing could be meant by “a great matter of state”:
The central government had ambitions and debts but almost no tax base of its own — because the taxes were still being collected by the domains. That is the fiscal pressure behind the reform, in the words of the man who ran the ministry.

title: 30  Inoue Kaoru’s account
text:
一體政府が舊幕府から受繼いだ領土は八百萬石であつて、その歲入を大蔵省で集めても、實際些細にして使用に窮する。… それより急務なものは無い。


The government as a whole has inherited eight million koku of territory from the former bakufu, but even when the Ministry of Finance collects this revenue, it is in reality so trivial that we struggle to put it to use. … There is no more urgent matter than this.

title: 30  Inoue Kaoru’s account
text:
30.2 The planning council, 9 July 1871

[七月九日]…のタ五時から九段上の木戸邸に於て廃藩置縣斷行の會議が開かれた。之に會したのは、公及び西郷兄弟・大久保・山縣・大山等で…知藩事を免官する一條は、その薨京を俟つて之を發令し…


On 9 July, from 5 p.m., a council was convened at Kido’s residence atop Kudan-zaka to decide on carrying out the abolition. Those gathered were Inoue, the Saigō brothers, Ōkubo, Yamagata, Ōyama, and others. … As for dismissing the domain governors, it was decided that the decree would be issued only after they had assembled in Tokyo.

Timing the decree for when the lords were in Tokyo — away from their home territories and retainers — was a deliberate choice, made five days before the edict.

title: 30  Inoue Kaoru’s account
text:
30.3 How it would be enforced
In a later recollection, Inoue described the assurances he sought before the decree — the Imperial Guard to back it, the finances to settle it, and a shared resolve to resign together if the court nobles blocked it:

御親兵となつて遣付けて… 金の方は宜いかと云ふ論。御請合ひ申すと云ふ。… 結局が付かぬ時分には貴所方どうするか… それは決心して皆袖を聯ねて退かう。


“We will use the Imperial Guard (御親兵) to enforce it. … ‘Is the money side in order?’ — they assured me it was. … ‘And when the [court council’s] debate drags on without conclusion, what will you all do?’ … the resolve was: ‘Then we will all join sleeves and withdraw’ [resign together].”

title: 30  Inoue Kaoru’s account
text:
30.4 What it shows
Inoue’s account supplies the half the diaries leave implicit: the reform rested on a fiscal motive (a revenue-starved center), a careful plan (issue the decree when the lords were gathered and the finances were guaranteed), and a credible fallback (the Imperial Guard, and the threat of a mass resignation inside the government). It is the practical underside of the bargain told in The bargain of 1871.

Source: 『世外井上公伝 (Sekkai Inoue-kō Den)』vol. 1, 第三編第三章第一節「廃藩置縣」, book pp. 449–452 (the authorized biography of Inoue Kaoru, 1933–34).

title: 31  The Boshin punishment decrees
text:
31.1 The punishment decree
Meiji 1 / 12 — December 1868 to January 1869
After the Boshin War, the new government punished the domains that had led the losing Northern Alliance. The decrees are worth reading because of what they did not do: they reduced and relocated the losing houses rather than destroying them — a precedent that shaped how the 1871 abolition and the 1884 peerage treated the same houses later.
The leaders were spared their lives; six major houses lost their domains; Yonezawa was cut down. Severe, but bounded.

title: 31  The Boshin punishment decrees
text:
○詔シテ松平容保及ヒ其黨與ヲ罰ス。乃チ特旨ヲ以テ松平容保、松平喜徳、林忠崇ノ死一等ヲ減シニ永禁錮ニ處シ、伊達慶邦、酒井忠篤、南部利剛、丹羽長國、阿部正靜、牧野忠訓ノ封土ヲ没シ謹愼セシメ、上杉齊憲ノ封土四萬石ヲ削リ…


An imperial decree to punish Matsudaira Katamori [Aizu] and his confederates. By special imperial intent: Matsudaira Katamori [Aizu], Matsudaira Yoshinori [Kuwana], and Hayashi Tadataka [Jōzai] have their death sentences commuted by one degree to permanent house arrest; Date Yoshikuni [Sendai], Sakai Tadazumi [Shōnai], Nanbu Toshikatsu [Morioka], Niwa Nagakuni [Nihonmatsu], Abe Masashizu [Shirakawa], and Makino Tadakuni [Nagaoka] have their fiefs confiscated and are placed under house arrest; Uesugi Narinori [Yonezawa] has 40,000 koku cut from his fief …

title: 31  The Boshin punishment decrees
text:
31.2 Reduction and relocation
Within days, reduced versions of the punished houses were put back — often on land taken from another punished house:

十七日。南部利剛ノ子恭ヲ以テ其先祀ヲ承カシメ磐城ノ地十三萬石ヲ賜ヒ。白石ニ移治ス


Day 17: Nanbu Toshikatsu’s son is permitted to inherit the family rites, granted 130,000 koku of Iwaki territory, and relocated to Shiraishi.


十五日。酒井忠篤ノ弟…ヲ以テ其先祀ヲ承カシメ。岩代ノ地十二萬石ヲ賜ヒ若松ニ移治シ


Day 15: Sakai Tadazumi’s brother is permitted to inherit the family rites, granted 120,000 koku of Iwashiro land, and relocated to Wakamatsu — the former Aizu castle town.

So the Morioka-Nanbu heir was reseated on the southern strip carved off Sendai, and a Sakai cadet from the punished Shōnai house was installed in the old Aizu seat. The same logic appears across the smaller cases.

title: 31  The Boshin punishment decrees
text:
31.3 What it shows
The pattern is reduction and relocation, not extinction. A government that had every reason to erase its enemies instead left them standing, smaller — and often gave them each other’s confiscated land to administer. That instinct — accommodate the losing side rather than write it out — runs forward through the whole settlement, down to the count rank the head of the defeated Sendai-Date house received in the 1884 peerage. The narrative is in The road to abolition; the alignment data is in Boshin alignment.

Source: 修史局編『明治史要 (Meiji Shiyō)』本紀 vol. 1, Meiji 1/12 entries, book pp. 128–132. National Diet Library Digital Collections. (Several smaller-domain name readings in the original are not fully legible at current scan resolution and are omitted here; the major houses above are verified.)

title: 32  The stipend buyout
text:
32.1 The proclamation
Daijōkan Decrees Nos. 108 & 109 — 5 August 1876
After 1871 the former lords and samurai were paid stipends out of the central treasury, replacing their lost domain incomes. In 1876 the government closed that out: the stipend settlement (chitsuroku shobun 秩禄処分) converted every stipend into a one-time government bond. This is the financial half of the 1871 settlement.
The accompanying ordinance opens by making the conversion universal — no protected class:

title: 32  The stipend buyout
text:
○第百八號… 家祿賞典祿ノ儀永世一代ノ年限等ヲ以テ給與有之候處其制限ヲ改メ來明治十年ヨリ別紙條例ノ通公債證書ヲ以テ一時ニ下賜候條此旨布告候事


No. 108. The hereditary stipends [家祿] and merit stipends [賞典祿], previously granted with hereditary or lifetime duration, are hereby changed: from Meiji 10 [1877] onward, they shall be paid out all at once, by means of public-debt bond certificates.



第一條　華士族及ヒ平民トモ各自ノ家祿賞典祿給與ノ制限ヲ改メ一時ニ之ヲ下渡スコト爲ニ以テ公債證書ヲ付與スヘシ


Article 1. Whether the recipient is kazoku, shizoku, or commoner, regardless of category, each person’s stipend grant is hereby changed, and to redeem it at one time, public-debt bond certificates shall be conferred.

title: 32  The stipend buyout
text:
32.2 The terms
The conversion table tilted against the largest stipends in two ways at once. The payout ran from five years’ purchase for the biggest stipends (≥70,000 yen) up to about fourteen years’ worth for the smallest; the interest rate ran from 5% on the largest bonds up to 7.5% on the smallest. The combined effect compressed the old distribution: the heads of the great houses, with stipends in the tens of thousands of yen, sat in the least generous tier.
The bonds could not be cashed out at will. The principal was locked for five years, then retired by annual lottery over thirty:

第六條　此公債證書ノ元金ハ五ヶ年間之ヲ据置キ六ヶ年目ヨリ大藏省ノ都合ニ因テ毎年抽籤ノ方法ヲ以テ三十ヶ年間之ヲ消却スルコトトス


Article 6. The principal of these bond certificates shall be held for five years; from the sixth year, at the Ministry of Finance’s convenience, by annual lottery, they shall be amortized over thirty years.

A companion decree issued the same day went further still — at the outset, the bonds could not even be traded:

○第百九號… 金禄公債證書ノ儀ニ追テ指令ニ及フ迄ハ賣買約定取結候儀ハ禁止候條此旨布告候事


No. 109. Regarding the bond certificates [of No. 108]: until further instructions, concluding any purchase or sale agreement in them is prohibited.

(The prohibition was lifted about two years later, around the bonds’ actual issuance in 1878.)

title: 32  The stipend buyout
text:
32.3 What it shows
The settlement paid the old elite off, but on terms the state controlled: universal, compressed against the largest holders, locked into a thirty-year schedule, and folded into the government’s general debt. Reneging would have meant defaulting on the state’s own borrowing. The timing was contested inside the government — Kido Takayoshi wanted it paired with a peerage, and was overruled — and the peerage did not arrive until 1884. The narrative is in The settlement.

Source: 太政官布告 第108号・第109号 (Daijōkan Decrees Nos. 108 & 109), Meiji 9/8/5 (5 August 1876), “金禄公債證書發行條例,” in 『法令全書』明治9年, book pp. 147–151. National Diet Library Digital Collections, pid 787956 (Public Domain Mark).

title: 33  The peerage decree
text:
33.1 The decree
Kanpō No. 307 — 7 July 1884 (gazetted 8 July)
The peerage ordinance (kazoku-rei 華族令) of 1884 established the five-rank peerage and sorted the old and new elites into it. It is the status half of the 1871 settlement — the durable, hereditary honor that bound the former-daimyō families and the Restoration’s architects to the new regime.
The Five Ranks are kō-kō-haku-shi-dan (公侯伯子男): prince, marquess, count, viscount, baron.

title: 33  The peerage decree
text:
朕惟フニ華族勳冑ハ國ノ藩屏ナリ宜シク授爵スルニ榮爵ヲ以テシ用テ寵光ヲ示スヘシ文武諸臣中興ノ偉業ヲ翼贊シ國ニ大勞アル者宜シク均シク優列ニ陞シ用テ殊典ヲ昭ニスヘシ茲ニ五爵ヲ叙テ其有禮ヲ秩ス…


On reflection: the kazoku and meritorious lineages are the bulwark of the nation. It is fitting that We bestow honored titles upon them, to show imperial favor. Civilian and military officials who have assisted the great enterprise of the Restoration, those who have rendered great service to the nation, should all alike be raised to the higher ranks, that their special honors be made manifest. Hereby We establish the Five Ranks [五爵] and order their proper hierarchy.
— Meiji 17 / 7 / 7 (7 July 1884)

title: 33  The peerage decree
text:
33.2 Two grounds for ennoblement
The decree names two distinct reasons for a title in one sentence — and the peerage was built on both:

Lineage — “the kazoku and meritorious lineages are the bulwark of the nation.” The former daimyō and court houses were ranked by who they were. The four leading domains sat at the top: Shimazu (Satsuma) and Mōri (Chōshū) as princes, Nabeshima (Saga) and Yamauchi (Tosa) as marquesses. How these ranks were assigned, and the cases that deviate from the rule, are in the peerage chapter and the nineteen deviations.
Merit — “officials who have assisted the great enterprise of the Restoration.” The men who had run the Restoration and the new state were ranked by what they had done. Of the sixteen senior-merit counts created in 1884, all sixteen came from the four leading domains — seven from Satsuma, six from Chōshū, two from Tosa, one from Saga (Yamagata, Itō Hirobumi, Kuroda, Inoue, and the rest).

The coalition that abolished the domains in 1871 supplied the entire top of the merit peerage in 1884.

title: 33  The peerage decree
text:
33.3 What it shows
The peerage made the settlement permanent. It was hereditary, and within five years — under the 1889 constitution — princes and marquesses held automatic seats in the House of Peers. At that point the government could not strip kazoku status without dismantling part of its own legislature. It also reached the losing side: the head of the defeated Sendai-Date house received a count rank under the ordinary lineage formula, listed among the non-penalized houses (see the Aizu–Sendai walkthrough). The narrative is in The settlement.

Source: 『官報』第三百七號 (Kanpō No. 307), Meiji 17/7/8 (8 July 1884), page 1 (the imperial decree, dated 7 July) and pages 3–4 (the ennoblement roster). National Diet Library Digital Collections, pid 2943511 (Public Domain Mark).

title: Bibliography
text:
Primary sources
This page aggregates every published source the dataset draws on. For per-row citations, see the matching source columns in the analytic table.
藩制一覧 (Hansei Ichiran, “Overview of Han Institutions”). 1870. Two-volume government survey of all daimyō (大名) domains, compiled in the immediate pre-haihan chiken (廃藩置県) period. National Diet Library Digital Collections. — The primary han-level source underlying every Hansei Ichiran-derived field.
官報 (Kanpō, “Official Gazette”) 第307号 (Kanpō #307). 1884-07-08. The Imperial government gazette publishing the 1884-07-07 kazoku (華族) ennoblement decree and initial 509-house roster. NDL Digital Collections, pid 2943511. — Primary citation for the 1884 kazoku* rank.*
官報 第308号 (Kanpō #308). 1884-07-09. Continuation of the same gazette with the late-cohort ennoblements (kuge (公家), etc.). NDL pid 2943512.
明治史要 (Meiji Shiyō, “Outline of Meiji History”). 1870s. Compendium compiled by the Bureau of Historiography (修史局, Shūshikyoku). — Primary source for the Meiji Shiyō population, household-count, and status-class breakdown fields, and for the verbatim 1871 haihan-chiken imperial decree (vol 1 pp. 27–28) used by the focal-han tier columns.
大隈重信関係資料「府藩県石高人口表」 (Ōkuma Shigenobu kankei shiryō: Fu-han-ken kokudaka jinkō-hyō, “Ōkuma Shigenobu Papers: Prefecture-Domain-County Kokudaka and Population Table”). Waseda University Library archival, call number イ14-A4361, Meiji 2 (1869). — Primary source for the Ōkuma uchidaka and population fields.
統計集誌 (Tōkei Shūshi, “Statistical Journal”). Compiled by 呉文聡 from 野村文夫 materials. — Primary source for the Tōkei Shūshi population field.
世外井上公伝編纂会編『世外井上公伝』全5巻 (Sekkai Inoue-kō Den Compilation Committee (ed.), Sekkai Inoue-kō Den, “Authorized Biography of Inoue Kaoru”). 1933–34. — Vol 1 pp. 449–453 (Ch. 3 §1 廃藩置縣) gives Inoue Kaoru’s actor-perspective on haihan-chiken (1871) including the pre-decree negotiations, the July 9 Kudan-zaka implementation council, Inoue’s 1909 Kyō-Meikai retrospective, and the verbatim two-tier focal-domain commendation (Sat-Chō-Saga-Tosa + Kumamoto/Nagoya/Tokushima/Tottori). Used by the focal-han tier columns.
木戸孝允『木戸孝允日記』(English: The Diary of Kido Takayoshi, trans. Sidney Devere Brown & Akiko Hirota, University of Tokyo Press, 1983). — Vol II pp. 65–67 contains Kido’s diary entries for 28–31 August 1871 (Meiji 4/7/13–16) on haihan-chiken, including his retrospective claim to having originated the 郡県制 reform, the “fiefs from Tokugawa-enemy” stratagem, the focal-four sequential persuasion narrative, and the rejected “hereditary governor” compromise. Vol III pp. 344–347 contains the 3–7 August 1876 entries on the chitsuroku-shobun debate (Kido vs Itō). Used by the focal-han tier columns and the chitsuroku-shobun chapter.
大久保利通家編『大久保利通文書』全10巻 (Ōkubo Toshimichi Family (ed.), Ōkubo Toshimichi Monjo, “Ōkubo Toshimichi Documents”). 日本史籍協會, 1927–29. — Authoritative chronological compilation of Ōkubo’s correspondence, 1851–1878. Vol 1 covers the pre-Restoration period (Kaei 4 / 1851 → Keiō 3 / Oct 1867); entry 124’s reference document is Ōkubo’s hand-copy of the Satsuma-Tosa Treaty (薩土盟約, Keiō 3/6/14 = July 1867), the founding political compact of the Sat-Chō-Tosa coalition that became the focal four (with Hizen joining) in 1871. Vol 4 (1928, NDL pid 1075767) covers Meiji 3/9 → Meiji 5; 卷二十一 (Internal Vol 21) contains entries 623–630 + the 利通日記抄 diary excerpts for Meiji 4/7 — Ōkubo’s contemporaneous record of the 7/9 Kudan-zaka implementation council and the 7/14 day-of-decree entry. The 1928 editorial 【辨説】 commentary documents the Kido vs Ōkubo intra-oligarchy disagreement on the 4-han cabinet balance. Vols 2, 3, 5 also acquired via NDL Digital Collections.
太政官布告 第108号 + 第109号 (Daijōkan-fukoku Nos. 108 & 109). 1876-08-05. The legal-government primary for the chitsuroku-shobun (秩禄処分). No. 108 = 金禄公債證書発行条例 (Hereditary-Stipend Public-Bond Certificate Issuance Ordinance) — the 7-article ordinance establishing the bond conversion schedule (regressive multipliers × progressive interest), 5-year grace + 30-year lottery amortization. No. 109 (same day) authorizes secondary-market trading. Located in 法令全書 明治9年 (NDL pid 787956, PDM, internet-public), book pp. 147–151. — The chitsuroku-shobun legal primary, parallels Kanpō #307’s role for the kazoku-rei. Sat-Chō-Saga-Tosa former daimyō all sit in the least-generous conversion tier (≥30k yen → 5–6 years’ worth × 5% interest).
法令全書 (Hōrei Zensho, “Compendium of Laws and Ordinances”), 内閣官報局 (Cabinet Printing Bureau), Meiji 9 vol. = 1876 ed. NDL pid 787956. — Canonical compiled-laws series; contains all 太政官布告 + 太政官達 of Meiji 9. Source for the chitsuroku-shobun decrees above.

title: Bibliography
text:
Secondary scholarly sources — Japanese
浅見雅男『華族誕生: 名誉と体面の明治』 (Asami Masao, Kazoku tanjō: meiyo to taimen no Meiji, “Birth of the Peerage: Honor and Reputation in Meiji”). リブロポート, 1994. — Backs 94% of the 1884 kazoku-rank citations. Articulates the genmai* (現米) threshold rule that reduces apparent rank-deviations from 81 (kokudaka-based) to 19 (genmai-based).*
華族大鑑刊行会編『華族大鑑』 (Kazoku Taikan Kankōkai (ed.), Kazoku taikan, “Compendium of the Peerage”). 1990. — Primary scholarly source for the dataset’s prince-rank ennoblements (3 rows).
工藤威『奥羽列藩同盟の基礎的研究 (Ōu reppan dōmei no kisoteki kenkyū, “Basic Research on the Northern Alliance”)』. 岩田書院, 2002. — Authoritative scholarly roster of the Northern Alliance (Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei), 31 members. Citable via the JP Wikipedia 奥羽越列藩同盟 article which transcribes the Kudō roster.
児玉幸多 ed.『藩史総覧 (Hanshi sōran, “Comprehensive Survey of Han History”)』. 新人物往来社, 1977. — Canonical han-classification reference for the Tokugawa-era category field (shinpan* (親藩) / fudai (譜代) / tozama (外様) / sankyō (御三卿) / tenryō (天領)).*
国史大辞典 (Kokushi daijiten, “Great Dictionary of National History”). Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1979–1997. — Definitive secondary on Japanese historical terminology + domain classifications.
小田部雄次『華族 近代日本貴族の虚像と実像 (Kazoku: kindai Nihon kizoku no kyozō to jitsuzō, “The Peerage: Image and Reality of the Modern Japanese Aristocracy”)』. 中公新書, 2006. — Secondary kazoku* reference, especially for small-domain edge cases.*
森岡浩『日本名門・名家大辞典 (Nihon meimon meika daijiten, “Great Dictionary of Famous Japanese Houses”)』. 東京堂出版, 2012. — Small-domain rank references.
松田敬之『〈華族爵位〉請願人名辞典 (Kazoku shakui seigan jinmei jiten, “Dictionary of Peerage-Title Petitioners”)』. 吉川弘文館, 2015. — Definitive 1884 exclusions catalog (kaieki* (改易) cases like 大沢家/堀江藩, 林家/請西藩).*
藤野保『佐賀藩』. 2010. — Saga-specific reference; supplies Saga’s navy, which Hansei Ichiran’s Volume 2 does not record (Saga’s army is in Table 9).

title: Bibliography
text:
Secondary scholarly sources — English
Beasley, W. G. The Meiji Restoration. Stanford University Press, 1972. — Standard scholarly history. Ch. XI for Boshin alignment + Principal Domains in 1860 (p. xii) for Tokugawa-era category assignments.
Esposito, Gabriele & Rava, Giuseppe. Japanese Armies 1868–1877: The Boshin War and Satsuma Rebellion. Osprey Men-at-Arms 530. Bloomsbury, 2020. — Military narrative + key Boshin combatants. Citable for the major focal-4 + Northern Alliance core.
Lebra, Takie Sugiyama. Above the Clouds: Status Culture of the Modern Japanese Nobility. University of Hawai’i Press, 1993. — Threshold-rule framing (kokudaka-based, which Asami 1994 corrects to genmai-based).
Ravina, Mark. Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan. Stanford University Press, 1999. — Appendix Table A1 ratios. Cited for the per-capita ratio fields. Known typo: Akita 久保田藩 gendaka* (現高)-per-commoner 0.049 should be 0.49 (decimal-place error).*
Craig, A. M. Chōshū in the Meiji Restoration. Harvard University Press, 1961 / 2000. — Chōshū-specific military supplement.
Roberts, Luke S. Mercantilism in a Japanese Domain: The Merchant Origins of Economic Nationalism in 18th-Century Tosa. Cambridge University Press, 1998. — Tosa supplement.
Jansen, Marius B. The Making of Modern Japan. Harvard / Belknap, 2000. — General history; also Jansen (1961/1994) Sakamoto Ryōma and the Meiji Restoration.
Drea, Edward J. Japan’s Imperial Army: Its Rise and Fall, 1853–1945. University Press of Kansas, 2009.
Jentschura, H., Jung, D. & Mickel, P. Warships of the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1869–1945. Naval Institute Press, 1977. — For Hansei Ichiran warship data verification.

title: Bibliography
text:
Data sources (not citable but documented)
Harvard CHGIS 1664 daimyō shapefile. — Used for the map polygons (approximation, since 1664 boundaries were generally stable through 1870).
bakumatsu.org web scrape. — Originally used as a finder for Tokugawa-era category, Boshin alignment, and last-lord identity. Not directly citable; values were cross-validated against Beasley 1972, 工藤 2002, 児玉 ed. 1977 and similar academic sources before being used in any analytic claim.
Wikipedia 府藩県三治制 scrape. — A web-scraped extract of the Japanese Wikipedia 府藩県三治制 article. Not directly citable; the article’s underlying primary sources (Meiji Shiyō, Ōkuma papers, Tōkei Shūshi) are the actual citations for each field.

title: Bibliography
text:
See also

data_provenance.md — per-column citation guidance, the single source of truth for which source backs which column.
Primary sources — the key documents behind the narrative (the abolition decree, the architects’ diaries and accounts, the Boshin punishment decrees, and the 1876 and 1884 laws), each presented in the original Japanese with an English working translation in this book’s Primary sources section.

All companion files will be released alongside the dataset.
